subject: Biology
q:
Identify the correct statements about cell biology?

  1. It is the study of genes.
  2. It is the study of organelles.
  3. It is the study of tissue growth.
skill: Define biology, and differentiate among the major divisions of biology through examples
feedback:
II and III only.Cell biology is that branch of biology which deals with the study of cells, its organelles and its cell division (which leads to tissue growth).

The study of genes is called genetics.

subject: Biology
q: At his lab, Dr. Adam classifies organisms into different groups and subgroups. Which branch of biology does he practice?
skill: Define biology, and differentiate among the major divisions of biology through examples
feedback:
Taxonomy is the branch of science that deals with the study and classification of organisms into groups.

A taxonomist is an individual who studies taxonomy. Plant Taxonomy is a branch of taxonomy that deals with the classification of plants

subject: Biology
q: The study of a new individual from a fertilised egg is done by
skill: Define biology, and differentiate among the major divisions of biology through examples
feedback: Embryology is the branch of biology that studies the prenatal development of gametes, fertilisation, and the development of embryos and fetuses. A scientist who studies embryology is known as an embryologist.

subject: Biology
q: The word biology is derived from two ______ words.
skill: Define biology, and differentiate among the major divisions of biology through examples
feedback: The word biology is derived from two Greek words; bio means life and logos means thought or reasoning .

subject: Biology
q:
Physical therapists study the biomechanics of how joints, muscles, and bones move during athletic activity. What branch(es) of science do they study?
I.   anatomy
II.  physiology
III. biochemistry
skill: Define biology, and differentiate among the major divisions of biology through examples
feedback:
Anatomy is the study of the structural organisation of living things, and physiology is how organisms, organ systems, organs, cells, and biomolecules carry out the chemical and physical functions that exist in a living system.
Biochemistry is the application of chemistry to the study of biological processes at the cellular and molecular level and is not as essential to physical therapists if they want to study the mechanics of joint and muscle movement.

subject: Biology
q: Which professional study of biology deals with the diagnosis and treatment of diseases of livestock?
skill: Relate biology to other branches of science and career paths taken
feedback:
Animal husbandry is the professional study of the diagnosis and treatment of diseases of livestock. Livestock includes all domestic animals.

Agriculture and farming are the science, art, or occupation concerned with cultivating land, raising crops, and feeding, breeding, and raising livestock. Fisheries is the academic study that draws on the study and investigation of fish processing, aquaculture and fishery resource management, fishing technology and management, fisheries environment and fisheries extension.

subject: Biology
q: What profession deals with the production of useful products through microorganisms?
skill: Relate biology to other branches of science and career paths taken
feedback: Biotechnologists study and modify living organisms to develop new useful products for the welfare of mankind. For example, the production of insulin from a microorganism or biofuels is managed by biotechnologists.

subject: Biology
q: Alina was studying the spread of the dengue virus. She gathered some data and made a graph. The graph shows that 75% of tropical regions were affected by this virus. What interdisciplinary field does her study fall under?
skill: Relate biology to other branches of science and career paths taken
feedback: Biomathematics is an interdisciplinary field in which you analyse data gathered from experimental work using mathematical techniques and tools to model biological processes.

subject: Biology
q: The following image will be studied under which interdisciplinary science?
skill: Relate biology to other branches of science and career paths taken
feedback:
The image shows the distribution of specific species across the world.

The interdisciplinary science that deals with the occurrence and distribution of different species of living organisms in different geographical regions of the world is known as biogeography.

subject: Biology
q: Which interdisciplinary science focuses on the cost value and profit value on a yield of maize?
skill: Relate biology to other branches of science and career paths taken
feedback: Bio-economics deals with the study of organisms from economical point of view. For example, the cost value and profit value of the yield of wheat, rice, and maize can be calculated through bio-economics. The benefits or losses can also be determined through bio-economics.

subject: Biology
q: A human is classified in the domain _____ and kingdom _____.
skill: Discuss the aims and principles of classification, and discuss binomial nomenclature
feedback: The Eukarya domain includes eukaryotes. Eukaryotes are organisms that have a well-defined nucleus. The domain Eukarya is further subdivided into kingdoms. These kingdoms include Protista, Fungi, Plantae, and Animalia.

subject: Biology
q:
Which domain(s) does the following cell belong to?
  1. Bacteria
  2. Archea
  3. Eukariya
skill: Discuss the aims and principles of classification, and discuss binomial nomenclature
feedback: The image given is of a prokaryotic cell. Prokaryotic cells fall into the domains Bacteria and Archaea. Eukaryotic cells make up the more familiar Domain Eukarya.

subject: Biology
q: If two organisms are in the same phylum, they must also belong to which of the following level of classification?
skill: Discuss the aims and principles of classification, and discuss binomial nomenclature
feedback: The classification of living things includes 7 organisation levels: kingdom, phylum, classes, order, families, genus, and species. The broadest classifications fall under domain and kingdom; whereas the most specific classification is by genus and species. Therefore, if two organisms are in the same phylum, we can conclude that they both belong to the same kingdom because kingdom is at a higher organisation level of classification than phylum.

subject: Biology
q: _____ is considered the father of taxonomy.
skill: Discuss the aims and principles of classification, and discuss binomial nomenclature
feedback: Carl Linnaeus was a Swedish botanist who is considered the father of taxonomy. Taxonomy is the branch of biology that deals with classifying and naming living organisms.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following levels of classification determine an organism's scientific name?
skill: Discuss the aims and principles of classification, and discuss binomial nomenclature
feedback: Binomial nomenclature is used to name an organism, where the first word beginning with a capital letter is the genus of the organism and the second word beginning with a lower-case letter is the species of the organism. Scientific names are printed in italics. For example, human beings are scientifically known as Homo sapiens.

subject: Biology
q: Identify the name and level of organisation of the following image.
options: the pancreas, which is an organ
the muscle, which is a tissue
the stomach, which is an organ
skill: Describe bio-elements as the most basic level of biological organisation
feedback: The image shows intestines. These are vital organs in the gastrointestinal tract of our digestive system. Their functions are to digest food and to enable the nutrients released from that food to enter into the bloodstream. Our intestines consist of two major subdivisions: the small intestine and the large intestine.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following organs are part of the digestive system?
skill: Describe bio-elements as the most basic level of biological organisation
feedback: The mouth, stomach, liver, and intestines are organs that are part of the digestive system. Lungs are part of the respiratory system, the brain is the part of nervous system, and the heart is part of the circulatory system.

subject: Biology
q: Which level of biological organisation is the given image?
skill: Describe bio-elements as the most basic level of biological organisation
feedback: The image represents a population of cats. A population is defined as the same species of organisms located at the same place at the same time. For example, a forest may include many cats. All of these cats represent the population of cats in this forest.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following is the highest level of biological organisation?
skill: Describe bio-elements as the most basic level of biological organisation
feedback:
The biological organisation of living things arranged from the simplest to the most complex are:
organelle → cells→ tissues→ organs→ organ systems → organisms → populations → communities → ecosystem → → biosphere At the highest level of organisation, the biosphere is the collection of all ecosystems, and it represents the zones of life on earth. It includes land, water, and even the atmosphere to a certain extent.

subject: Biology
q: Which structure is the site of protein synthesis in a cell?
skill: Describe bio-elements as the most basic level of biological organisation
feedback: Each type of organelle is specialised to perform a specific function. Mitochondria are specialised for cellular respiration, centrioles are specialised for cell division in animals, and ribosomes are specialised for protein synthesis. However, a flagellum is a tail-like structure found in some cells that aids in the movement of the cell.

subject: Biology
q:
Which of the following is/are macromolecules?
  1. glucose
  2. starch
  3. protein
  4. lipid
skill: Describe bio-elements as the most basic level of biological organisation
feedback: Biomolecules are classified as macromolecules and micromolecules. Macromolecules are those molecules that have a high molecular weight like carbohydrates, starch, proteins, and lipids. Micromolecules have a low molecular weight, for example, glucose and water.

subject: Biology
q: Identify the correct hierarchy of the level of organisation in plants and animals?
options: organs &#8594 tissue &#8594 organ systems &#8594 cells &#8594 organism
cells &#8594 tissue &#8594 organs &#8594 organ systems &#8594 organism
organelles &#8594 organs &#8594 organ systems &#8594 cells &#8594 tissue
tissue &#8594 organelles &#8594 organs &#8594 organ systems &#8594 cells
skill: Describe bio-elements as the most basic level of biological organisation
feedback:
Multicellular organisms are made of many parts that are needed for survival. These parts are divided into different levels of organisation. Their order is:

cells → tissues → organs → organ systems → organism

Cells are the simplest level of organisation. They are the building blocks of all living organisms. Many cells working together form tissue. There are many different types of tissue in both plants and animals. When there are layers of tissue working together, they form an organ. All animals have organs. When organs work together, they form organ systems. Organ systems keep the body regulated and in a stable state. Different organ system combines to form a complete organism.

subject: Biology
q: A scientist notices that the organism X is made up of trillions of cells. What is organism X?
skill: Describe bio-elements as the most basic level of biological organisation
feedback:
Organism X is a fern. Multicellular living organisms like plants and animals are made up of more than one cell.

Bacteria and amoeba are known as unicellular organisms because they are made up of a single cell. Viruses are not living things. Viruses are complicated assemblies of molecules, including proteins, nucleic acids, lipids, and carbohydrates, but on their own they can do nothing until they enter a living cell. Without cells, viruses would not be able to multiply.

subject: Biology
q: Which group includes organisms have an absorptive mode of nutrition?
skill: Differentiate among the levels of organisation of life in multicellular organisms
feedback: Fungi obtain nutrients by decomposing dead organic matter. A saprotroph is an organism that obtains its nutrients from non-living organic matter by absorbing soluble organic compounds. That is why fungi have an absorptive mode of nutrition.

subject: Biology
q: When we study the feeding relations among different animal species of a forest, we are studying them at the level of organisation of the
skill: Differentiate among the levels of organisation of life in multicellular organisms
feedback: When we study the feeding relations among different animal species of a forest, we are studying at community level of organisation because a community is an assemblage of different populations, interacting with one another within the same environment.

subject: Biology
q: Members of the same species living in the same place at the same time comprise a
skill: Differentiate among the levels of organisation of life in multicellular organisms
feedback: A population is defined as a group of individuals of the same species living and interbreeding within a given area at a given time. Members of a population often rely on the same resources, are subject to similar environmental constraints, and depend on the availability of other members to persist over time.

subject: Biology
q: Choose a green alga found in water that shows colonial organisation.
skill: Differentiate among the levels of organisation of life in multicellular organisms
feedback: Volvox is a green alga found in water that shows colonial organization. It forms spherical colonies of up to 50,000 cells. They live in a variety of freshwater habitats and were first reported by Antonie van Leeuwenhoek in 1700.

subject: Biology
q: Which of these tissues also make the glandular tissue in animals?
skill: Differentiate among the levels of organisation of life in multicellular organisms
feedback: Organs are composed of tissues, which are in turn composed of cells. Epithelial tissue derived from endoderm is essentially glandular epithelium that make the glandular tissue in animals.

subject: Biology
q: A group of tissues doing a particular job is called a
skill: Differentiate among the levels of organisation of life in multicellular organisms
feedback: In higher multicellular organisms, more than one type of tissue having related functions are organised together and make a unit, called organ . Different tissues of an organ perform their specific functions and these functions collectively become the function/s of that organ.

subject: Biology
q: What is the correct sequence of the levels of organisation of life?
options: cell, organelle, molecule, organ, tissue, organ system, individual
molecule, tissue, organelle, cell, organ system, organ, individual
molecule, organelle, cell, tissue, organ, organ system, individual
organ system, organ, tissue, cell, molecule, organelle, individual
skill: Differentiate among the levels of organisation of life in multicellular organisms
feedback: The biological levels of organisation of living things arranged from the simplest to most complex are: molecule, organelle, cells, tissues, organs, organ systems, individual.

subject: Biology
q: Why do humans require many different types of cells to survive?
options: Human cells are specialised and work independently to perform different functions.
Human cells are identical and work independently to perform different functions.
Human cells are specialised and work together to perform a specific function.
Human cells are identical and work together to perform the same function.
skill: Define a cell and differentiate between unicellular and multicellular organisms - Biology
feedback: Humans are multicellular organisms. They require many different types of cells which are specialised to perform a specific type of function. The cells of the same type combine and perform a specific function for the body. For example, the cells of the tongue help taste the food we eat.

subject: Biology
q: Which statement describes bacteria?
options: It contains different kinds of cells.
It is a part of a larger organism.
It contains more than one cell.
skill: Define a cell and differentiate between unicellular and multicellular organisms - Biology
feedback: A bacterial cell acts as a unicellular organism. There are organelles in the cell which perform all life processes.

subject: Biology
q: A single-cell organism can do everything a cell from a multicellular organism can do except _____.
skill: Define a cell and differentiate between unicellular and multicellular organisms - Biology
feedback:
Cells in a multicellular organism are specialised while cells in a unicellular organism are generalised.

In a multicellular organism, different types of cells have different capabilities. Many cells of the same type combine to perform the same function in our body. Unicellular organisms can perform all necessary life processes for survival such as moving about, feeding, and respiration on their own.

subject: Biology
q: A scientist is trying to decide whether an organism is unicellular or multicellular. Which information would help the scientist most to make a decision?
options: How fast does the organism grow?
How many types of cells are in the organism?
What is the size of the cells in the organism?
skill: Define a cell and differentiate between unicellular and multicellular organisms - Biology
feedback: The information about the number and types of cells would help the scientist decide if an organism is multicellular or unicellular.

subject: Biology
q: In _____, the outer cells are specialised to face the environment, whereas the inner cells perform other functions.
skill: Define a cell and differentiate between unicellular and multicellular organisms - Biology
feedback:
Grass is a multicellular organism, therefore, it has specialised cells that perform different life processes.
The outer cells are specialised to face the environment and the inner cells carry out other functions.

A unicellular organism is composed of only one cell and that one cell performs all internal and external functions for its survival.

subject: Biology
q: The life span of a cell _____.
options: is longer in a unicellular organism
is longer in a multicellular organism
is the same in both multicellular and unicellular organisms.
skill: Define a cell and differentiate between unicellular and multicellular organisms - Biology
feedback: The life span of a cell is longer in a multicellular organism because all the cells are specialised to only do a specific job. Their workload is divided. Whereas, the hard-working single cell of a unicellular organism has such a heavy workload that it can only maintain a very short lifespan.

subject: Biology
q: _____ is a unicellular organism that has both animal-like and plant-like characteristics.
skill: Define a cell and differentiate between unicellular and multicellular organisms - Biology
feedback: Euglena has both animal-like and plant-like characteristics. It has chloroplast in its cell for photosynthesis much like plants do. However, unlike plants, it can move from one place to another with the help of a long whip-like structure, called flagellum. This ability to move makes it animal-like.

subject: Biology
q: A colony of unicellular organisms, Volvox, behaves as a single organism where one end of the colony develops the eyespots. The eyespot helps the colony to move toward a light source. How is the Volvox similar to a multicellular organism?
options: The groups of cells that make up the colony are identical.
The cells provide structural support to the whole colony.
The individual groups of cells in the colony perform different functions.
The cells in the colony provide food for one another.
skill: Define a cell and differentiate between unicellular and multicellular organisms - Biology
feedback: In a Volvox colony, the individual groups of cells in the colony perform different functions. This characteristic makes the colony similar to a multicellular organism. A multicellular organism has specialised cells where there is division of work.

subject: Biology
q: Bubbles is reading a story. She reads about an organism that can survive as a single cell. What is she most likely reading about?
skill: Differentiate between unicellular and multicellular organisms
feedback: A paramecium is a small unicellular organism. It's single celled body is capable of reproducing, digesting food, and moving about.

subject: Biology
q: Why do humans require many different types of cells to survive?
options: Human cells are specialised and work independently to perform different functions.
Human cells are identical and work independently to perform different functions.
Human cells are specialised and work together to perform a specific function.
Human cells are identical and work together to perform the same function.
skill: Differentiate between unicellular and multicellular organisms
feedback: Humans are multicellular organisms. They require many different types of cells which are specialised to perform a specific type of function. The cells of the same type combine and perform a specific function for the body. For example, the cells of the tongue help taste the food we eat.

options: It contains different kinds of cells.
It is a part of a larger organism.
It contains more than one cell.
skill: Differentiate between unicellular and multicellular organisms
feedback: A bacterial cell acts as a unicellular organism. There are organelles in the cell which perform all life processes.

skill: Differentiate between unicellular and multicellular organisms
feedback:
Cells in a multicellular organism are specialised while cells in a unicellular organism are generalised.

In a multicellular organism, different types of cells have different capabilities. Many cells of the same type combine to perform the same function in our body. Unicellular organisms can perform all necessary life processes for survival such as moving about, feeding, and respiration on their own.

options: How fast does the organism grow?

feedback: The information about the number and types of cells would help the scientist decide if an organism is multicellular or unicellular.

skill: Differentiate between unicellular and multicellular organisms
feedback:
Grass is a multicellular organism, therefore, it has specialised cells that perform different life processes.
The outer cells are specialised to face the environment and the inner cells carry out other functions.

A unicellular organism is composed of only one cell and that one cell performs all internal and external functions for its survival.

options: is longer in a unicellular organism

subject: Biology
q: What is meant by the word 'malaria'?
skill: Discuss the study of malaria as an example of the biological method
feedback: The word 'malaria' is derived from two Greek words. 'Mala' means 'bad' and 'aria' means 'air'. Together, it means the disease of bad air.

subject: Biology
q: Why do female mosquitoes need blood?
skill: Discuss the study of malaria as an example of the biological method
feedback: Female mosquitoes use two very different food sources. They need sugar for energy, which is taken from sources such as nectar. And they need the blood of mammals or birds so that they can nourish their child-bearing bodies. Blood is full of proteins and amino acids, which makes it the perfect prenatal supplement for the development of mosquito eggs . This is why only female mosquitoes drink blood.

subject: Biology
q: Who performed experiments on sparrows regarding malaria?
skill: Discuss the study of malaria as an example of the biological method
feedback: In 1898, Ronald Ross, an army surgeon working in India, carried out a series of carefully controlled experiments using Plasmodium-infected sparrows and Culex mosquitoes to demonstrate that malaria was transmitted through mosquito bites.

subject: Biology
q: How does Plasmodium enter the blood of an organism?
options: through the saliva of a female mosquito to the human bloodstream
through the blood of a female mosquito to the human bloodstream
through the saliva of a female mosquito to the human kidney
through the blood of a female mosquito to the human kidney
skill: Discuss the study of malaria as an example of the biological method
feedback: Plasmodium enters the bloodstream of an organism through an infected female Anopheles mosquito. When this mosquito sucks the blood of a healthy person, it secretes some of its saliva into the bloodstream to prevent clotting of her food canal. This saliva transmits the infectious agent Plasmodium.

subject: Biology
q: How was quinine first obtained?
skill: Discuss the study of malaria as an example of the biological method
feedback: Quinine is a drug obtained from the cinchona bark and is used chiefly in the treatment of malaria, an infection caused by the protozoan parasite Plasmodium. Malaria is transmitted to humans by the bite of various species of mosquitoes. This tree is native to Central and South America, as well as some islands in the Caribbean and western parts of Africa.

subject: Biology
q: If a test shows that a person has Plasmodium in his blood, but he does not show any symptoms of malaria, what hypothesis would you formulate on the basis of this information?
options: The person will get malaria within a few days.
The person will develop immunity to malaria.
The person will not get malaria but could still spread it to other people.
The person will never be infected with any disease.
skill: Discuss the study of malaria as an example of the biological method
feedback: When an infectious agent enters a body, it goes through an incubation period. That is, the period between the entry of a parasite in the host and the appearance of symptoms. The parasite takes time to affect its new environment, which is why the symptoms of malaria develop within 9 to 14 days following infection.

subject: Biology
q: Once malaria parasites enter a person's blood, then they travel to which organ?
skill: Discuss the study of malaria as an example of the biological method
feedback: The malaria parasites enter a person's bloodstream and travel to the liver. When the parasites mature, they leave the liver and infect red blood cells.

subject: Biology
q: How does a biologist arrive at a conclusion?
options: He guesses what the results should be.
He compares the data from the experimental results to the hypothesis.
He keeps carrying out different observation.
He looks at the hypotheses and chooses the one that makes the most sense to him.
skill: Explain the steps involved in the scientific method in detail - Biology
feedback: To draw a conclusion, a biologist gathers actual, quantitative data from experiments. Data for each of the groups are then averaged and compared statistically.

subject: Biology
q: Lying in bed, Sarah notices that she gets cold every time her brother opens the door. What step would this be in the scientific method?
skill: Explain the steps involved in the scientific method in detail - Biology
feedback: The first step of the scientific method is to make an observation about the world around you. Before a hypothesis can be made or experiments carried out, one must have something to hypothesise about. So, here, Sarah made an observation that she gets cold every time her brother opens the door.

subject: Biology
q:
A hypothesis must be testable to be scientifically valid.
Being testable means
options: Some observations could prove that the hypothesis is incorrect.
The opposite of the hypothesis if it is proven wrong.
The hypothesis can be proved or disproved.
There must be several factors in the hypothesis to choose from.
skill: Explain the steps involved in the scientific method in detail - Biology
feedback: A hypothesis is a tentative answer to a scientific question. A testable hypothesis is a hypothesis that can be proved or disproved as a result of testing, data collection, or experience. Only testable hypotheses can be used to conceive and perform an experiment using the scientific method.

subject: Biology
q:
Which of the following are characteristics of a hypothesis?
I.   consistent
II.  testable.
III. factual
skill: Explain the steps involved in the scientific method in detail - Biology
feedback: A hypothesis is a plausible explanation of any phenomenon based on some initial evidence. It has to be consistent and testable. A hypothesis is considered a fact only after it has been tested multiple times.

subject: Biology
q: Which one of the following is the correct sequence of the biological method?
skill: Explain the steps involved in the scientific method in detail - Biology
feedback: The basic steps of the scientific method starts with making observations, forming a hypothesis based on those observations, and then making deductions that one can test through experimentation.

subject: Biology
q: When is a biologist most likely to use deductive reasoning?
skill: Explain the steps involved in the scientific method in detail - Biology
feedback: Biologists use reasoning to formulate a hypothesis. They draw deductions from hypotheses. Deductions are the logical consequences of hypotheses. For this purpose, a hypothesis is taken as true and expected results (deductions) are drawn from it.

subject: Biology
q:
A gardener sees a large snake nearby. He knows that generally snakes sting, so the gardener ran away.
The gardener
skill: Explain the steps involved in the scientific method in detail - Biology
feedback: Deductions are the logical consequences of hypotheses. For this purpose, a hypothesis is taken as true that snake will bite the gardener and expected results (deductions) are drawn from it. So, the gardener ran away. Deductive reasoning moves from generalised principles to a specific conclusion.

subject: Biology
q: Choose a unicellular organism that causes malaria in mammals and birds.
skill: Explain the steps involved in the scientific method in detail - Biology
feedback: Malaria is caused by a one-celled parasite (unicellular organism) called a Plasmodium. Female Anopheles mosquitoes pick up the parasite from infected people when they bite to obtain blood needed to nurture their eggs. Inside the mosquito, the parasites reproduce and develop. When the mosquito bites again, the parasites contained in the salivary gland are injected and pass into the blood of the person being bitten

subject: Biology
q: How does genetic diversity affect the survival of certain species?
options: Less genetically diverse populations have higher species survival rates because mating is not limited.
Mutations that cause genetic diversity are always fatal and reduce species survival.
Genetically diverse populations are at higher risk for extinction because individuals are more susceptible to disease.
Genetic diversity allows some individuals to have traits that help them survive in times of environmental change.
skill: Define biodiversity, and discuss its importance
feedback:
The more genetically diverse a population is, the higher the chances that some members of the population will survive in times of environmental change or challenges.
For example, some members of a species might have a genetic variation that makes them resistant to a particular disease. This diversity allows those members to survive long enough to reproduce and ensure the progression of the species.

subject: Biology
q: Biodiversity found on earth today is the result of ____ years of evolution.
skill: Define biodiversity, and discuss its importance
feedback: Biodiversity is the result of 4 billion years of evolution. Science has been unable to accurately determine the origin of life. However, based on evidence, some scientists believe that life may have arisen a few hundred million years after Earth’s formation.

subject: Biology
q:
Which of the following statements is/are true regarding the relationship between genetic diversity and biodiversity?

I.   As genetic diversity increases, biodiversity decreases.
II.  Genetic diversity and biodiversity are equivalent to one another.
III. Biodiversity is not dependent on genetic diversity.
IV. Biodiversity decreases as genetic diversity decreases.
skill: Define biodiversity, and discuss its importance
feedback: A decrease in genetic diversity can cause a decrease in population. In turn, there would be more inbreeding, resulting in a reduction of the population size almost to extinction.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following is true regarding the global distribution of biodiversity?
options: Biodiversity is distributed evenly across the earth.
As temperatures decrease, biodiversity levels increase.
Areas at high elevations contain greater biodiversity than areas at low elevations.
Biodiversity is greatest closest to the equator and lessens as you move towards the north and south poles.
skill: Define biodiversity, and discuss its importance
feedback:
The climate near the equator is warm year-round and supports many species, so the biodiversity is rich. As you move further from the equator, the occurence of different seasons causes temperate regions to have less biodiversity. This is because organisms in these regions must adapt to multiple conditions.
Extreme regions with cold or dry conditions, such as the tundra and deserts, have the least biodiversity.

subject: Biology
q:
Which of the following statement(s) are ecological resources provided because of biodiversity?

I.   purification of water
II.  increased soil fertility
III. trash collection
skill: Define biodiversity, and discuss its importance
feedback: Biodiversity ensures the stability of ecosystems by reducing the dependence of one species on another for food, shelter, and even the maintenance of the physical environment. It protects water resources. It cleans it, controls the route it takes, thus reducing flooding risks. It also strengthens soil quality and increases soil fertility thus increasing potential crop yields. It does NOT, however, provide trash services.

subject: Biology
q: Biodiversity is measured by looking at
options: genetic variation, species richness, and diversity within an ecosystem.
location, habitat, and the environment of a specific species.
population, diseases, and gender difference in an ecosystem.
plant diversity, habitat productivity, and availability of natural resources.
skill: Define biodiversity, and discuss its importance
feedback: Biodiversity is the variety of life on earth. It can be measured by determining the genetic variation, species richness, and diversity within an ecosystem.

subject: Biology
q: Biodiversity provides us with
skill: Define biodiversity, and discuss its importance
feedback: Biodiversity is important for the security of all our resources. It is essential for ensuring that we have, for example, clean water, timber, paper, fibre, and food.

subject: Biology
q: In some cases, different organisms are known by the same common name. The name blackbird is used for crows and for what other organism?
skill: Discuss the aims and principles of classification, and discuss binomial nomenclature
feedback: Common names of organisms in local languages have no scientific basis. Crows and ravens are often called blackbirds across North America.

subject: Biology
q: Brassica niger is the scientific name of black mustard. What can be deduced from this name?
options: Brassica is the order and niger is the species of the mustard plant.
Brassica is the genus and niger is the species of the mustard plant.
Brassica is the phylum and niger is the class of the mustard plant.
Brassica is the subclass and niger is the family of the mustard plant.
skill: Discuss the aims and principles of classification, and discuss binomial nomenclature
feedback: The first word of the scientific name represents the genus and the second word represents the species. So, in this case Brassica is the genus and niger is the species of black mustard plant.

subject: Biology
q: The logical classification of taxonomic information is called _____.
skill: Discuss the aims and principles of classification, and discuss binomial nomenclature
feedback: The branch of biology that deals with classifying taxonomic information is called systematics.

subject: Biology
q: Identify the only natural taxonomic category from the following:
skill: Discuss the aims and principles of classification, and discuss binomial nomenclature
feedback:
The only natural taxonomic category is species. For example, if we look at the flowers in a meadow, the seashells on a beach, or the birds in a garden we will rapidly notice that they divide up into distinct types that don't interbreed with each other.
A species can be defined as a plant, animal, bacteria, fungus, or any other living thing that when mated with another member of the same species produces a fertile offspring.

subject: Biology
q: In what taxonomic hierarchy are humans classified?
options: animalia → chordata → mammalia → primates → Hominidae → homo → H. sapiens
animalia → mammalia → chordata  → primates → Hominidae → homo → H. sapiens
animalia → chordata → mammalia  → Hominidae → primates → homo → H. sapiens
animalia → Hominidae → chordata  → primates → mammalia → homo → H. sapiens
skill: Discuss the aims and principles of classification, and discuss binomial nomenclature
feedback:
Classification, or taxonomy, is a system of categorising living things. There are seven divisions in the system. The order of human classification is as follow:
  • kingdom - Animalia
  • phylum - Chordata
  • class - Mammalia
  • order - Primates
  • family - Hominidae
  • genus - Homo
  • species - H. sapiens

subject: Biology
q: A human is classified in the domain _____ and kingdom _____.
skill: Discuss the aims and principles of classification, and discuss binomial nomenclature
feedback: The Eukarya domain includes eukaryotes. Eukaryotes are organisms that have a well-defined nucleus. The domain Eukarya is further subdivided into kingdoms. These kingdoms include Protista, Fungi, Plantae, and Animalia.

subject: Biology
q:
Which domain(s) does the following cell belong to?
  1. Bacteria
  2. Archea
  3. Eukariya
skill: Discuss the aims and principles of classification, and discuss binomial nomenclature
feedback: The image given is of a prokaryotic cell. Prokaryotic cells fall into the domains Bacteria and Archaea. Eukaryotic cells make up the more familiar Domain Eukarya.

subject: Biology
q: If two organisms are in the same phylum, they must also belong to which of the following level of classification?
skill: Discuss the aims and principles of classification, and discuss binomial nomenclature
feedback: The classification of living things includes 7 organisation levels: kingdom, phylum, classes, order, families, genus, and species. The broadest classifications fall under domain and kingdom; whereas the most specific classification is by genus and species. Therefore, if two organisms are in the same phylum, we can conclude that they both belong to the same kingdom because kingdom is at a higher organisation level of classification than phylum.

subject: Biology
q: Organisms in which kingdom absorb nutrients from other living things?
skill: Describe classification and the different systems of classification
feedback: Kingdom Fungi consists of organisms that are heterotrophic. Such organisms rely on other living organisms for their food. Fungi decompose organic matter to fulfil their nutritional needs.

subject: Biology
q: Organisms that can synthesise their own food should be classified in kingdom _____.
skill: Describe classification and the different systems of classification
feedback: Organisms that can synthesise their own food should be classified in kingdom Plantae. The kingdoms Protista, Animalia, and Fungi only include organisms that CANNOT synthesise their own and depend on other organisms for nutrition.

subject: Biology
q: In which kingdom is strep bacteria, shown in the image, included?
skill: Describe classification and the different systems of classification
feedback: Streptococcus is a genus of gram-positive coccus or spherical bacteria that belongs to the kingdom Monera.

subject: Biology
q: Who suggested the first classification system?
skill: Describe classification and the different systems of classification
feedback: Aristotle developed the first system of classification of animals. He classified all living organisms known at that time into either the group Plantae or in Animalia.

subject: Biology
q: A scientist is doing his research on prokaryotes and unicellular eukaryotes. In which kingdom would he classify prokaryotes?
skill: Describe classification and the different systems of classification
feedback: The scientist should classify prokaryotes in kingdom Monera. This kingdom includes all unicellular organisms that lack a nucleus (prokaryotes).

subject: Biology
q: Scientists have developed a medicine that destroys cell walls. They discovered that this medicine is effective against bacteria. Would it work on viruses as well?
options: Yes, viruses and bacteria have the same structures.
Yes, viruses and bacteria both make people sick.
No, viruses only have plasma membranes.
No, viruses do not have cell walls.
skill: Describe the structure of a virus and explain why it cannot be categorised with the five-kingdoms
feedback: A virus particle (virion) does not have a cell wall, like prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells. It has a protein coat that encases the nucleic acid (DNA or RNA). So, the medicine will not have any effect on viruses.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following is a non-living characteristic of viruses?
options: their ability to cause diseases in a host
their ability to multiply only inside a host
skill: Describe the structure of a virus and explain why it cannot be categorised with the five-kingdoms
feedback: Viruses are on the border of living and non-living things. One of the properties shared by viruses with non-living things is their crystalline nature. All the other options are the properties of a living cell.

subject: Biology
q:
Which of the following characteristics of life do viruses have?
  1. They have genetic material.
  2. They reproduce themselves
  3. They are made up of cells
skill: Describe the structure of a virus and explain why it cannot be categorised with the five-kingdoms
feedback: Viruses are obligate parasites which means they can’t survive on their own and need host cells to reproduce. They have some important features in common with cell-based life. For instance, they have the genetic material based on the same genetic code used in our cells and the cells of all other living creatures. They can only replicate by infecting a host cell and therefore cannot reproduce on their own. They are not made out of cells. A single viral particle, known as a virion, consists solely of genetic material surrounded by capsid, a protein coat. Some viruses are further protected by an envelope.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following labelled structures does the virus use to protect itself?
skill: Describe the structure of a virus and explain why it cannot be categorised with the five-kingdoms
feedback: Structure B and C perform the role of protecting the virus. Structure B is an envelope that protects the virus. Structure C is a capsid, a protein coat that protects the viral genetic material and helps infect new cells. Structure A is the genetic material (a strand of DNA/RNA) of a virus. The strand is considered the core of the virus and is necessary for its replication.

subject: Biology
q: What distinguishes prions from viruses?
skill: Describe the structure of a virus and explain why it cannot be categorised with the five-kingdoms
feedback: Prions can be distinguished from viruses based on whether any genetic material is present or absent. Prions are smaller than viruses and their aggregates can only be observed through an electron microscope. Viruses contain genetic material and proteins. The genetic material can either be DNA or RNA.

subject: Biology
q: A scientist is investigating a specimen in a laboratory. Which of the following would allow her to conclude that it is NOT a virus?
options: the specimen contains DNA and RNA
the specimen has a protein coat
skill: Describe the structure of a virus and explain why it cannot be categorised with the five-kingdoms
feedback: If the specimen contains both DNA and RNA then, the scientist can conclude that the specimen is not a virus. Viruses contain only DNA or RNA as their genetic material, never both.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following is most responsible for increased carbon dioxide in the atmosphere?
skill: Discuss conservation for the sustainability of nature
feedback: Fossil fuels contain large amounts of carbon, and when burned for energy, they release large amounts of carbon dioxide as a by-product. An increase in photosynthetic activity in plants would result in a decrease of atmospheric carbon dioxide, as that is a reactant in the process of photosynthesis. Ocean acidification and the melting of polar ice caps occurs as a result of the increase in atmospheric carbon dioxide, but they are not the cause of it.

subject: Biology
q:
Which of the following statements explain how an invasive species can threaten biodiversity in a geographic region?
I.   It can cause damage to the environment.
II.  It can harm the human economy.
III. It may compete with native species for food and habitat.
skill: Discuss conservation for the sustainability of nature
feedback: Invasive species are introduced species that become well-established in their new environment, spread easily and cause ecological and economic harm. They frequently outcompete native species and lead to the decline or even extinction of natives such as different types of plants, animals, fungi, and microorganisms.

subject: Biology
q:
Which of the following points regarding extinction is true?
I.   The biodiversity of the earth has remained constant because speciation and extinction rates are always equal.
II.  Extinctions caused by human activity are higher than the natural extinction rate.
III. Extinction occurs only as a result of natural disasters, such as asteroids or volcanic activity.
skill: Discuss conservation for the sustainability of nature
feedback:
Biodiversity is affected by the balance between the speciation and extinction rates, and these are not always equal. When the extinction rate exceeds the speciation rate, biodiversity experiences a decline. Historically, the natural extinction rate is between 1-5 extinctions per year. The impact of human activities has caused this rate to jump to a significantly higher rate, offsetting the balance of biodiversity.
Although natural disasters can cause extinction events to occur, human activities have had a massive impact on the extinction rates on the planet.

subject: Biology
q: Which human activity would most likely deplete non-renewable resources?
options: using natural predators to eliminate pests
skill: Discuss conservation for the sustainability of nature
feedback: If human population continues to grow at an exponential rate, non-renewable resources (ex: fossil fuels) are bound to run out. However, regulating industrial pollution, developing wildlife sanctuaries and using natural predators to eliminate pests would have a positive impact on the environment.

subject: Biology
q: An electron microscope can magnify a specimen by a factor of _____.
topic: Microscopy and the emergence of cell theory
skill: Compare the light microscope and the electron microscope
feedback: An electron microscope can magnify a specimen by a factor of 250000. The magnification of a microscope tells us the number of times it can enlarge an object without any blurriness. The magnification of an electron microscope is 250000X.

subject: Biology
q: A light microscope can magnify a specimen by a factor of _____.
topic: Microscopy and the emergence of cell theory
skill: Compare the light microscope and the electron microscope
feedback: A light microscope can magnify a specimen by a factor of 1500. The magnification of a microscope tells us the number of times it can enlarge an object without any blurriness. The magnification of a light microscope is 1500X.

subject: Biology
q: The resolving power of an electron microscope is _____.
topic: Microscopy and the emergence of cell theory
skill: Compare the light microscope and the electron microscope
feedback: The resolving power of an electron microscope is 0.2 nanometre. In simpler terms, it means that an electron microscope cannot distinguish objects smaller than 0.2 nanometre. Due to this high resolving power, we can study the organelles of cells such as nucleus, mitochondrion, and chloroplast.

subject: Biology
q: The resolving power of a light microscope is _____.
topic: Microscopy and the emergence of cell theory
skill: Compare the light microscope and the electron microscope
feedback: The resolving power of a light microscope is 0.2 micrometre. In simpler terms, it means that a light microscope cannot distinguish objects smaller than 0.2 micrometre. For example, a light microscope cannot be used to study the internal structures of bacteria as their size is less than 0.2 micrometre.

subject: Biology
q: There are _____ micrometres (\[\mu\]m) in one millimetre (mm).
topic: Microscopy and the emergence of cell theory
skill: Compare the light microscope and the electron microscope
feedback:
A millimetre is a metric unit and equal to one thousandth of a meter. It is used widely to measure small distances in engineering. The abbreviation is mm.

A micrometre is a metric unit and is also used for the precise measurement of small distances in engineering. There are 1000 micrometres in a millimetre. The abbreviation is \[\mu\]m.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following is NOT a postulate of cell theory?
options: A cell is the basic structural and functional unit of life.
All living organisms are composed of cells.
Cells can arise through spontaneous generation.
All cells arise through the division of previously existing cells.
topic: Microscopy and the emergence of cell theory
skill: Trace the development of cell theory
feedback: The cell theory does NOT state that cells can arise through spontaneous generation. The cell theory has three postulates. First, a cell is the basic structural and functional unit of life. Second, all living organisms are composed of cells. And finally, that all cells arise only through the division of previously existing cells.

subject: Biology
q: Why are viruses not classified as cells?
options: Viruses are not classified as cells because they lack nucleic acids.
Viruses are not classified as cells because they lack proteins.
Viruses are not classified as cells because they lack metabolism.
Viruses are not classified as cells because their genetic material is always RNA.
topic: Microscopy and the emergence of cell theory
skill: Trace the development of cell theory
feedback: Viruses are not classified as cells because they lack metabolism. Viruses, along with prions, are considered acellular particles since they can't carry out living processes on their own and instead rely on host cells to reproduce.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following doesn't consist of cells?
topic: Microscopy and the emergence of cell theory
skill: Trace the development of cell theory
feedback:
Prions do not consist of cells. Prions are simple infectious proteins that cause disease that promoting misfolding of proteins.
Prions, along with viruses, are considered acellular particles since they can't carry out metabolism on their own and instead rely on host cells to reproduce.

subject: Biology
q: Robert Hooke discovered cells using _____.
topic: Microscopy and the emergence of cell theory
skill: Trace the development of cell theory
feedback: Robert Hooke discovered cells using a light microscope. He observed the cork tissue of plants under his microscope. He noticed tiny empty compartments which he termed as cells.

subject: Biology
q: Which scientist proposed that "all living cells arise from pre-existing cells"?
topic: Microscopy and the emergence of cell theory
skill: Trace the development of cell theory
feedback: In 1855 Rudolf Virchow, a German physician, proposed an important extension of cell theory. He proposed that all living cells arise from pre-existing cells ('omnis cellula e cellula").

subject: Biology
q: Small cells are generally _____ efficient in exchanging nutrients compared to larger cells.
skill: Calculate and relate surface area to volume ratio to find out its relationship with cell size
feedback: Small cells are generally more efficient in exchanging nutrients compared to larger cells. The surface area to volume ratio of smaller cells is higher than larger cells. This means that equal surface area of both cells exchanges nutrients for different amounts of volume of cells. In the small cells, the same amount of surface area regulates less volume of cell compared to larger cells.

subject: Biology
q: As the surface area to volume ratio of cells decreases, their size _____.
skill: Calculate and relate surface area to volume ratio to find out its relationship with cell size
feedback: As the surface area to volume ratio of cells decreases, their size gets larger. There is an inverse relation between the size of cells and its surface area to volume ratio. Cells that are smaller in size have less volume but more surface area. Therefore, the ratio of surface area to volume of small cells is relatively large.

subject: Biology
q: As the surface area to volume ratio of cells increases, their size _____.
skill: Calculate and relate surface area to volume ratio to find out its relationship with cell size
feedback: As the surface area to volume ratio of cells increases, their size gets smaller. There is an inverse relation between the size of cells and its surface area to volume ratio. Cells that are larger in size have more volume but less surface area. Therefore, the ratio of surface area to volume of large cells is relatively small.

subject: Biology
q: The radius of a human egg cell is 0.1 µm. What is its surface area to volume ratio?
skill: Calculate and relate surface area to volume ratio to find out its relationship with cell size
feedback:
The surface area to volume ratio of any sphere equals \frac{3}{r}. In this question, we are given:

Radius of a human egg cell: r = 0.1 µm Surface area of a sphere: S.A = 4pr^{2} Volume of a sphere: V = \frac{4}{3}pr^{3} We have to calculate the ratio of surface area to volume ratio of a human egg cell which equals: \begin{align} \frac{S.A}{V} &= \frac{4pr^{2}}{{4/3}pr^{3}} \\ &=\frac{3}{r} \\ &=\frac{3}{0.1} \\ &=30 \mbox{ µm}^{-1}\\ \end{align} Therefore, the ratio of surface area to volume of a human egg cell equals 30\;{ µm}^{-1}.

subject: Biology
q: The volume and surface area of a sphere are calculated using the equation \frac{4}{3}pr^{3} and 4pr^{2}, respectively. What is the ratio of surface area to volume of a spherical cell?
skill: Calculate and relate surface area to volume ratio to find out its relationship with cell size
feedback:
In this question, we are given:

Surface area of a sphere: S.A = 4pr^{2} Volume of a sphere: V = \frac{4}{3}pr^{3} We have to calculate the ratio of surface area to volume ratio of a spherical cell which equals: \begin{align} \frac{S.A}{V} &= \frac{4pr^{2}}{{4/3}pr^{3}} \\ &=\frac{3}{r} \\ \end{align} Therefore, the ratio of surface area to volume of a spherical cells equals \frac{3}{r}.

subject: Biology
q: All these organisms have cell walls EXCEPT
skill: Discuss cellular structures and their functions
feedback: Animals are multicellular, and move with the aid of cilia, flagella, or muscular organs based on contractile proteins. They have organelles including a nucleus, but no chloroplasts or cell walls.

subject: Biology
q: Which of these materials is NOT a component of the plasma membrane?
skill: Discuss cellular structures and their functions
feedback: The principal components of the plasma membrane are lipids (phospholipids and cholesterol), glycoprotein, and carbohydrate groups that are attached to some of the lipids and proteins. The DNA , however, is present in the nucleus.

subject: Biology
q: The _____ is a membrane-enclosed structure in eukaryotic cells that contains the DNA of the cell.
skill: Discuss cellular structures and their functions
feedback: The nucleus is an organelle found in eukaryotic cells. Inside its fully enclosed nuclear membrane, it contains the majority of the cell's genetic material. This material is organised as DNA molecules, along with a variety of proteins, to form chromosomes.

subject: Biology
q: Plant cells have _____ and _____, which are not present in animal cells.
skill: Discuss cellular structures and their functions
feedback: Plant cells have chloroplasts, a cell wall and a central vacuole. Animal cells lack these three organelles. Plant cells have chloroplasts because they make their own food. Plant cells have a cell wall so that they do not burst when the central vacuole fills up with water.

subject: Biology
q: The mitochondria function in _____.
skill: Discuss cellular structures and their functions
feedback: Mitochondria are tiny organelles inside cells that are involved in releasing energy from food. This process is known as cellular respiration. It is for this reason that mitochondria are often referred to as the power house of the cell.

subject: Biology
q: Ribosomes are constructed in the _____.
skill: Discuss cellular structures and their functions
feedback: Eukaryotic ribosome synthesis is especially complicated, because the ribosomal proteins themselves are made by ribosomes in the cytoplasm (outside of the nucleus), so they then must be imported into the nucleolus for assembly onto the nucleolus-derived rRNA.

subject: Biology
q: The plasma membrane of a cell does NOT
options: act as a boundary or border for the cytoplasm.
regulate the passage of material in and out of the cell.
contain a network of lipids and proteins.
skill: Discuss cellular structures and their functions
feedback: All cells are surrounded by a plasma membrane. The membrane is composed of a phospholipid bilayer arranged back-to-back. The membrane is also covered in places with cholesterol molecules and proteins. The plasma membrane is selectively permeable and regulates which molecules are allowed to enter and exit the cell. It doesn't contain any hereditary material.

subject: Biology
q: Which is the main component of the prokaryotic cell wall?
skill: Discuss cellular structures and their functions
feedback: The major component of the prokaryotic cell wall is peptidoglycan or murein. This rigid structure of peptidoglycan, specific only to prokaryotes, gives the cell shape and surrounds the cytoplasmic membrane.

subject: Biology
q: A centrosome consists of _____ centrioles.
skill: Discuss cellular structures and their functions
feedback: Animal cells have \[2\] centrioles located near the exterior surface of the nucleus. The two centrioles are collectively called a centrosome.

subject: Biology
q: Many epithelial cells in animals have the capacity to secret mucous and other chemical compounds. Which of the following organelle must be present abundantly in them?
skill: Relate the structure of specialised cells with their function
feedback:
Epithelial cells must contain Golgi apparatus abundantly. Golgi apparatus is an organelle that is primarily involved with secretion and intracellular transport. Since epithelial cells secrete mucous and other chemical compounds, they must have a high number of Golgi apparatus.
These epithelial cells must lack chloroplast and cell wall as they are animal cells. They must contain a nucleus however; the nucleus is not involved with the secretion of mucous or other chemical compounds.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following cells have a high number of mitochondria?
skill: Relate the structure of specialised cells with their function
feedback:
Muscle cells have a high number of mitochondria to allow movement in organisms. The number of mitochondria in any cell depends on its metabolic activity and energy requirements. Cells that are metabolically active and have high energy demands, contain mitochondria in great numbers. For example, liver cells have hundreds or even thousands of mitochondria because their function requires the expenditure of large amounts of energy.
Outer skin cells, hair cells, and red blood cells are not metabolically active and don't have high energy requirements.

subject: Biology
q: Muscle tissues help humans move because they have the capacity to _____.
skill: Relate the structure of specialised cells with their function
feedback: Muscles have the capacity to both contract and pull. In fact, muscles help in movement because they can pull by contracting themselves. Muscles lack the capacity to push, they can only pull on something.

subject: Biology
q: How are root hair cells adapted to absorbing water and salts from the soil?
options: because root hair cells have a high surface area to volume ratio
because root hair cells have a high volume to surface area ratio
because root hair cells have a low surface area to volume ratio
because root hair cells lack chloroplasts
skill: Relate the structure of specialised cells with their function
feedback:
Root hair cells are adapted to absorb water and salts from soil because they have a high surface area to volume ratio. They are long cells that penetrate deep into the soil. As a result, they are highly specialised for absorbing water and salts.
Root hair cells lack chloroplasts however; this doesn't contribute towards their ability to absorb water and salts.

subject: Biology
q: What characteristic of red blood cells allows them to carry haemoglobin?
options: both round shape and red colour
skill: Relate the structure of specialised cells with their function
feedback:
The round shape red blood cells (RBCs) allows them to carry haemoglobin.
Haemoglobin is a globular protein that binds to oxygen and delivers it to all parts of the body through red blood cells. The globular shape of haemoglobin is crucial for its ability to bind to oxygen. Thus, the round shape of red blood cells ensures the smooth transport of oxygen to different parts of the body.
The red colour of RBCs and blood is due to the haem group in haemoglobin. RBCs have a lifespan of four months however; this doesn't contribute towards their ability to carry haemoglobin.

subject: Biology
q: Red blood cells lack a nucleus so that they can _____.
options: carry as much oxygen as possible
give the blood its red colour
skill: Relate the structure of specialised cells with their function
feedback:
Red blood cells (RBCs) lack a nucleus so that they may carry as much oxygen as possible. Due to the absence of a nucleus, RBCs have more space available. This is because they play a huge role in transporting oxygen to the entire body.
RBCs don't divide as they lack a nucleus and therefore, can't transfer any information to the next generation of cells. The red colour of blood is due to the haem group present in haemoglobin. The lifespan of RBCs is between 100-120 days.

subject: Biology
q: Asad touched a steaming hot cup of tea and quickly pulled his arm away. Which of the following are involved in this response?
topic: Compare major animal tissues in terms of their cell specifications, locations and functions
feedback: All the options are correct. In this case, the receptors present in the skin sense the high temperature of the cup and send the message to the spinal cord through neurons. The nervous tissue in the spinal cord first orders the muscle tissue in your arm to quickly withdraw and then sends an 'update' to the brain. Thereby, Asad experiences pain.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following muscle types is always  voluntarily controlled?
topic: Compare major animal tissues in terms of their cell specifications, locations and functions
feedback:
Only skeletal muscles are always voluntarily controlled. Such muscles are attached to bones and allow movement and locomotion.

Smooth muscles are found in the walls of the alimentary canal, blood vessels, etc. They ensure the movement of substances within the body. They are involuntary in action.
Cardiac muscles are found in the heart and are responsible for producing a continuous heartbeat. They are also involuntary in action.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following tissues is found on our skin?
topic: Compare major animal tissues in terms of their cell specifications, locations and functions
feedback: Stratified squamous epithelium consists of many layers of flat cells. a single layer of flat cells. It is present in the lining of the oesophagus, mouth, and over the skin. The multiple layers of the stratified squamous epithelium provide structural integrity and protect the skin and internal organs against possible wear-and-tear.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following tissues is found in our kidneys?
topic: Compare major animal tissues in terms of their cell specifications, locations and functions
feedback: Cuboidal epithelium consists of a single layer of cube-shaped cells. It is found in kidney tubes, and small glands such as salivary glands. It is responsible for the secretion of hormones and enzymes.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following tissues is found in the reproductive system of humans?
topic: Compare major animal tissues in terms of their cell specifications, locations and functions
feedback: Columnar epithelium consists of a single layer of elongated cells. It is found in the reproductive system, the alimentary canal, and gall bladder, among others.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following is an example of connective tissue?
topic: Compare major animal tissues in terms of their cell specifications, locations and functions
feedback:
Adipose tissue is a type of connective tissue that is found around kidneys, under the skin, and in the abdomen. It provides energy and supports organs.

Skeletal and cardiac tissue is a type of muscle tissue while nervous tissue is neurons found in the brain and spinal cord.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following tissues is found in our lungs?
topic: Compare major animal tissues in terms of their cell specifications, locations and functions
feedback:
Squamous epithelium consists of a single layer of flat cells. It is found in the lungs, heart, and blood vessels. Here, it allows the movement of materials across it.
Stratified squamous epithelium is present in the lining of the oesophagus, mouth, and over the skin.
Columnar epithelium is found in the alimentary canal, and gall bladder, among others.
Cuboidal epithelium is found in kidney tubes, and small glands.

subject: Biology
q:
Last month, Sana checked her body weight and it was 38 kgs. She started exercising and the next time she checked her weight, it was 45 kgs. Which of the following statement(s) correctly describes the reason for her weight gain?
  1. Exercise increases the number of skeletal muscles.
  2. Exercise increases the amount of fat in the body.
  3. Exercise develops more muscle.
topic: Compare major animal tissues in terms of their cell specifications, locations and functions
feedback: Exercise does not increase the number of skeletal muscles or the amount of fat in the body. It simply burns fat and develops muscle instead. Muscle, although it takes less space than fat, is heavier. Therefore, even though Sana may not look fat, her weight would be higher.

subject: Biology
q: In an accident, Hamid had a spinal cord injury and was paralysed. Which of the following tissues would likely be damaged in this injury?
topic: Compare major animal tissues in terms of their cell specifications, locations and functions
feedback:
Nerve tissues are found in the brain, spinal cord, and nerves. If the spinal cord is damaged through a spinal cord injury, it can result indamage to nerve tissues, which can further cause a disruption of signals to areas of the body. In some cases, this can cause paralysis.

Epithelial tissue is found in the outer layer of the skin. Smooth tissue is found in alimentary canals, the urinary bladder, and blood vessels, while endothelial tissues line the inside of hearts and blood vessels.

subject: Biology
q:
Which of the following is/are involuntary muscles?
  1. skeletal muscles
  2. cardiac muscles
  3. smooth muscles
topic: Compare major animal tissues in terms of their cell specifications, locations and functions
feedback: Smooth muscles and cardiac muscles are involuntary in action i.e. their contraction is not under the control of our will while skeletal muscles are voluntary in action.

subject: Biology
q: The following image is an example of ______.
topic: Compare major animal tissues in terms of their cell specifications, locations and functions
feedback: The above image is a cartilage, which is a form of connective tissue . Connective tissue provides both strength and elasticity to certain parts of the body, such as the ears. Epithelial tissue, skeletal tissue, and cardiac tissue do not have a rubbery matrix. They are rigid and act as protective and/or supportive barriers.

subject: Biology
q: Which muscle type is correctly matched to its location?
topic: Compare major animal tissues in terms of their cell specifications, locations and functions
feedback: The columnar epithelium has elongated cells. It is found in the alimentary canal and gall bladder, where it releases secretions. The squamous epithelium is found in the lungs. The cuboidal epithelium is found in kidney tubes and the ciliated columnar epithelium is present in the trachea and bronchi and releases mucous.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following tissues forms outer layers in animals only?
topic: Compare major animal tissues in terms of their cell specifications, locations and functions
feedback: Epithelial tissues line the outer surfaces of organs and blood vessels throughout the body. An example is an epidermis, the outermost layer of the skin. These tissues are only found in animals. Epidermal tissues are present in both plants and animals. Endothelial tissues line the inside of the heart and blood vessels and neural tissues are found in the brain, spinal cord, and nerves.

subject: Biology
q: A person's body is unable to move food through the digestive system. According to the diagram, which muscle(s) is/are not working properly?
topic: Compare major animal tissues in terms of their cell specifications, locations and functions
feedback: Muscle A is a smooth muscle, which allows food to easily move through the digestive tract. However, muscle B is a cardiac muscle and makes up the heart. C is a skeletal muscle that forms bones.

subject: Biology
q: Ali is trying to identify an unknown tissue. He determines that its cells are striated and that it contains many nuclei. What type of tissue is he most likely looking at?
topic: Compare major animal tissues in terms of their cell specifications, locations and functions
feedback: Skeletal muscle tissue have striated (striped or streaked) cells and contain many nuclei. They are responsible for the movements of bones. Smooth muscle tissue consists of smooth, non-striated cells, each with a single nucleus. Cardiac muscle tissue is also striated, but it has a single nucleus in each cell. It is also known as involuntary muscles.

subject: Biology
q: The major cells of ground tissue are _____.
topic: Compare major animal tissues in terms of their cell specifications, locations and functions
feedback: In plants, parenchyma is one of the three main types of ground tissue. It is the most common ground tissue and can be distinguished through its thin cell wall as compared to other cells. Parenchyma cells make up the bulk of the soft parts of plants, including the insides of leaves, flowers, and fruits (but not the epidermis or veins of these structures).

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following statement best explains diffusion?
options: The diffusion of particles takes place against a gradient of concentration until the particles are distributed evenly in space.
The diffusion of particles takes place along a gradient of concentration until particles are distributed evenly in space.
The diffusion of particles takes place against a gradient of concentration until all of the particles are concentrated at one end.
The diffusion of particles takes place along a gradient of concentration until all of the particles are concentrated at one end.
topic: Passage of molecules into and out of cells
skill: Compare diffusion, facilitated diffusion, osmosis, endocytosis and exocytosis
feedback: During diffusion, particles will move along a concentration gradient from a higher concentration region to a lower concentration region to reach equilibrium (that is, until they are equally distributed in space). This process does not require any energy.

subject: Biology
q: The figure below shows a beaker filled with water and solute. The beaker has a semi-permeable membrane in the middle. Which of the following explains what will happen?
options: water will flow from side B to side A
water  will flow into side A to side B
the solute and solvent will separate entirely
topic: Passage of molecules into and out of cells
skill: Compare diffusion, facilitated diffusion, osmosis, endocytosis and exocytosis
feedback: Water flows into side A because a semi-permeable membrane only allows liquid to flow through. Solute molecules cannot cross through. Water moves from an area with a low concentration of solute to an area of high concentration of solute (in other words, an area of high concentration of water to an area with a low concentration of water ). Since the solute concentration in B is lower than the solute concentration in A, water moves from side B to the side A to balance out the concentration gradient called osmosis.

subject: Biology
q: An animal cell has 2% salt concentration. Which of the following is most likely to happen if the animal cell is placed in a solution of 0.05% salt concentration?
options: Water will move out of the animal cell which will cause it to shrink.
Water will move into the animal cell which will cause it to expand and burst.
Water will enter inside the animal cell which will cause it to shrink.
Water will move out of the animal cell which will cause it to expand and burst.
topic: Passage of molecules into and out of cells
skill: Compare diffusion, facilitated diffusion, osmosis, endocytosis and exocytosis
feedback: The concentration of water in the salt solution is greater than the animal cell, therefore, water will move into the animal cell. Since animal cells lack a cell wall, the osmotic pressure will expand the cell resulting in its lysis.

subject: Biology
q: By which process does the gaseous exchange in lungs occur?
topic: Passage of molecules into and out of cells
skill: Compare diffusion, facilitated diffusion, osmosis, endocytosis and exocytosis
feedback:
Diffusion is the movement of molecules from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration.
It is a principle method by which substances move within cells, as well as across cell membranes. Carbon dioxide, oxygen, and glucose, for example, can cross cell membranes by diffusion. The exchange of gas in gills and lungs occurs by this process.

subject: Biology
q: The process by which small molecules are forced to move across semi-permeable membranes with the aid of water or blood pressure is called _____.
topic: Passage of molecules into and out of cells
skill: Compare diffusion, facilitated diffusion, osmosis, endocytosis and exocytosis
feedback:
Active transport is a process by which small molecules are forced to move across semi-permeable membranes with the aid of water or blood pressure.

For example, in the body of an animal, blood pressure forces water and dissolved molecules to move through the semi-permeable membranes of the capillary wall cells.

subject: Biology
q: When would a cell need to use active transport instead of passive transport?
options: to transport ions with a high internal cell concentration out of the cell using channels
to take in nutrients against the concentration gradient
to diffuse oxygen through the cell membrane
to move water to the external environment
skill: Compare passive transport with active transport
feedback: Active transport moves particle lower concentration to higher concentration by the help of ATP when taking in nutrients against the concentration gradient. However, the moving of ions along the concentration gradient, osmosis, diffusion, and movement of water are all forms of passive transport.

subject: Biology
q:
Which of the following correctly tells the characteristic(s) of active transport?
  1. A partially permeable membrane is required.
  2. Energy is required.
  3. It occurs against a concentration gradient.
skill: Compare passive transport with active transport
feedback: Active transport is an energy-driven process which is used to move particles against a concentration of gradient across a semipermeable membrane. Active transport relies on protein channels embedded in a cell membrane to occur.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following substances can't be diffused through the plasma membrane?
skill: Compare passive transport with active transport
feedback: Although <b>sodium ions are small<b>, their charges prevent them from moving across the cell membrane through simple diffusion. They must be transported by special proteins through the process of <u>active transport</u>. However, an oxygen and carbon dioxide exchange occurs by diffusion down a concentration gradient. The movement of water involves osmosis.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following statements explains how the sodium-potassium pump works?
options: Potassium cannot attach to the pump until sodium ions detach from the pump.
After ATP hydrolysis occurs, group of phosphate attaches to pump before the sodium ions.
Sodium and potassium ions bind to the pump at the same time, and then sodium is released.
A sodium-potassium pump process begins when potassium attaches to the pump, which hydrolyses ATP.
skill: Compare passive transport with active transport
feedback:
The sodium-potassium pump uses active transports to moves the substance from higher to lower concentration. The pump moves sodium and potassium ions in opposite directions. This pump uses energy in the form of ATP. For each molecule of ATP that the pump uses, three sodium ions move out the cell and two potassium ions move into the cell.
The pump starts in its inward-facing form where it can bind to sodium ions. After three sodium ions bind to the pump, they trigger the pump to hydrolyse ATP and phosphorylate it. This phosphorylation causes it to change its shape so that it is outward-facing and ready to bind to two potassium ions.

subject: Biology
q: How can substances be transported across a membrane against their concentration gradient?
options: with the help of a hydrophobic plasma membrane
with the help of a plasma membrane that is permeable to small molecules
with the help of membrane proteins that are ATP-dependent carrier molecules
some membrane proteins act as channels for specific molecules to enter a cell
skill: Compare passive transport with active transport
