feedback: To move a substance against a concentration gradient, the cell uses energy in the form of ATP(adenosine triphosphate) during active transport.

subject: Biology
q: In a hypertonic environment, the shrinking of the cytoplasm of plant cells is called _____.
skill: Describe the phenomenon of turgidity in cells and how it changes
feedback: In a hypertonic environment, the shrinking of the cytoplasm of plant cells is called plasmolysis. Due to a lower water concentration outside the cell, the water molecules will move from the plant cells out into the hypertonic solution, resulting in plasmolysis.

subject: Biology
q: Freshwater protozoa are unicellular microorganisms that carry out osmoregulation through organelles called contractile vacuoles. These vacuoles must be responsible for removing excess _____.
skill: Describe the phenomenon of turgidity in cells and how it changes
feedback: The contractile vacuoles freshwater protozoa are responsible for removing excess water. These unicellular microorganisms live in a freshwater environment, which has higher water concentration compared to salts. Therefore, they have to constantly expel excess water that may move into the cells through osmosis.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following cells will undergo plasmolysis if placed in a hypertonic solution?
skill: Describe the phenomenon of turgidity in cells and how it changes
feedback:
Guard cells will undergo plasmolysis if placed in a hypertonic solution. Only plant cells are plasmolysed when placed in a hypertonic solution. Due to a lower water concentration outside the cell, the water molecules will move from the guard cells out into the hypertonic solution, resulting in plasmolysis.
Animal cells when placed in a hypertonic solution don't undergo plasmolysis but instead shrink. Nephrons, red blood cells, and hepatocytes (liver cells) are all examples of animal cells.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following cells will undergo lysis if placed in a hypotonic solution?
skill: Describe the phenomenon of turgidity in cells and how it changes
feedback: Neurons will undergo lysis if placed in a hypotonic solution. Due to a higher water concentration outside the cell, the water molecules will move into the cell through osmosis causing the neurons to burst. Only animal cells burst when placed in a hypotonic environment. Plant cells, on the other hand, have cell walls that resist bursting. Instead, the water molecules exert pressure at the cell wall resulting in turgor pressure. Palisade mesophyll cells, phloem cells, and guard cells are all examples of plant cells.

subject: Biology
q: _____ is the movement of water molecules from a region of higher concentration to a region of lower concentration across a partially permeable membrane.
skill: Describe the phenomenon of turgidity in cells and how it changes
feedback: Osmosis is the movement of water molecules from a region of higher concentration to a region of lower concentration across a partially permeable membrane. The direction of osmosis depends on the gradient potential of water.

subject: Biology
q: Chromosomes condense and become visible during the _____.
skill: Describe the process of mitosis in plant and animal cells
feedback: Chromosomes are evenly spread out in a cell. However, when mitosis occurs the chromosomes condense at point and become visible in the prophase.

subject: Biology
q: Each chromosome pair divides into a pair of identical chromatids jointed one another by the ______.
skill: Describe the process of mitosis in plant and animal cells
feedback: Chromatids are identical and are joined to one another by the centromere in the middle.

subject: Biology
q: _____ are two tiny structures in an animal cell, located in the cytoplasm near the nuclear membrane.
skill: Describe the process of mitosis in plant and animal cells
feedback: Centrioles are located near the nucleus in an animal cell. Centrioles move towards the poles of the cell during cell division. As the cell undergoes cell division, the spindle fibres emerge out from the centrioles.

subject: Biology
q: The final act of dividing in mitosis is called ____.
skill: Describe the process of mitosis in plant and animal cells
feedback: The division of the cell at the end of mitosis is made possible by cytokinesis. Cytokinesis is the division of cytoplasm. The cytoplasm is pinched in half to create two identical daughter cells.

subject: Biology
q: ______ is a constantly occurring process in the human body.
skill: Describe the process of mitosis in plant and animal cells
feedback: Mitosis is always occurring in a body. Living beings replace old cells with new and healthy ones through mitosis.

subject: Biology
q: What are the pairs of chromosomes called?
skill: Describe the process of meiosis in animal cells
feedback: Chromosomes that are similar are called homologous chromosomes.

subject: Biology
q: A cell has undergone meiosis-II, but it has not split into four. What will be the next step in the process of cell division to split the cell further?
skill: Describe the process of meiosis in animal cells
feedback: Cytokinesis is the division of cytoplasm. A cell that undergoes meiosis II, it divides into four cells with half the number of chromosomes as the parent cell.

subject: Biology
q: Sister chromatids are attached at the centromere and align at the centre of the cell with the help of a spindle fibre. How are the sister chromatids separated from each other?
options: Spindle fibres shorten and move the chromatids towards the poles.
Spindle fibres elongate and move towards the centre of the cell.
skill: Describe the process of meiosis in animal cells
feedback: In anaphase-II, the sister chromatids are pulled apart as the spindle fibres shorten. Additionally, the ends of the cells are pulled further apart.

subject: Biology
q: Our characteristics are determined by the _____.
skill: Describe the process of meiosis in animal cells
feedback: Individual characteristics are determined by crossing-over during meiosis-I. While crossing-over, the sister chromatids exchange chromosomal material. This process results in a variety of genetic combinations that lead to variation among individuals of a same family.

subject: Biology
q: In which phase of meiosis is the tetrad of chromosomes formed?
skill: Describe the process of meiosis in animal cells
feedback: Prophase is the longest phase in meiosis. During this stage, chromatin condenses into chromosomes. The homologous chromosomes line up with each other and form pairs by a process called synapsis. Each pair of homologous chromosomes is called a bivalent. Each bivalent has four chromatids, so it may also be called a tetrad.

subject: Biology
q: What type of cell division is responsible for producing variation in the next generation and what is its mechanism?
options: Mutations in mitosis leads to variation in the next generation.
Meiosis I leads to variation in the next generation through the process of crossing over.
Crossing over in meiosis II leads to variation in the next generation.
Synapsis in meiosis II leads to variation in the next generation.
skill: Compare mitosis and meiosis
feedback:
During meiosis I, synapsis takes place in which homologous chromosomes line up with each other and form pairs. This allows the homologous chromosomes to exchange segments with one another in a phenomenon called crossing over. This exchange of segments is directly responsible for producing variation in the next generation.
Both synapsis and crossing over occur only in meiosis I. Any changes that occur in mitosis aren't passed down the generation as it takes place only in somatic cells.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following statements is TRUE?
options: Mutations that may occur during meiosis II are passed down to the offspring.
Mutations that may occur during mitosis are harmless.
Mutations that may occur during meiosis can cause cancer.
Mutations may occur in meiosis II, but not in mitosis.
skill: Compare mitosis and meiosis
feedback: Both mitosis and meiosis are prone to mutations. However, the impact of these mutations is different for each type of cell division. Since meiosis takes place only in germ cells, any mutation that occurs is passed down to the offspring. However, mutations in mitosis aren't passed down to the offspring because mitosis only occurs in somatic cells. Mutations in mitosis can lead to cancer unlike mutations in meiosis.

subject: Biology
q: How is meiosis II different from mitosis?
options: Meiosis II doesn't involve the duplication of chromosomes, but mitosis does.
Mitosis halves the number of chromosomes in daughter cells but meiosis II doesn't.
Meiosis II occurs in somatic cells and mitosis takes place in germ cells.
Mutations may occur in meiosis II but not in mitosis.
skill: Compare mitosis and meiosis
feedback:
To halve the number of chromosomes in the daughter cells in meiosis II, it doesn't involve the duplication of chromosomes. If the chromosomes are duplicated in meiosis II, then the number of chromosomes is not halved.

All the other options are factually incorrect. Mitosis maintains the number of chromosomes in the daughter cells. Both mitosis and meiosis are prone to mutations. Gametes are produced by the process of meiosis taking place in germ cells only.

subject: Biology
q: Why is meiosis II similar to mitosis?
options: Because they both involve the splitting at the centromeres.
Because they both result in halving of the chromosome number.
Because they both involve pairing of homologous chromosomes.
Because they both involve lining up of the bivalents along the equator.
skill: Compare mitosis and meiosis
feedback: Both meiosis II and mitosis involve the separation of sister chromatids during anaphase, which must require the splitting at the centromeres. Neither results in halving of the chromosome number.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following occurs in meiosis but NOT in mitosis?
options: homologous chromosomes are sorted into different nuclei
homologous chromosomes line up along the equator
sister chromatids are held together at the centromere
skill: Compare mitosis and meiosis
feedback: During meiosis I, homologous chromosomes are sorted into different daughter nuclei.One daughter nucleus receives the maternal chromosome, while the other daughter nucleus receives the paternal chromosome. This does not occur in mitosis, where the daughter nuclei receive both the paternal and maternal chromosomes of each homologous pair.

subject: Biology
q: Catabolism and anabolism are both types of _____.
skill: Define metabolism, and differentiate between its types with examples
feedback: Metabolism includes the biochemical reactions that maintain life in living organisms. It includes both anabolic and catabolic reactions. Anabolic reactions use energy to produce large molecules, whereas catabolic reactions break down large molecules to release energy.

subject: Biology
q: What is released during catabolism?
skill: Define metabolism, and differentiate between its types with examples
feedback: Catabolism includes the biochemical reactions that break down larger molecules, which releases energy.

subject: Biology
q: Which language is the term metabolism derived from?
skill: Define metabolism, and differentiate between its types with examples
feedback: The term metabolism is derived from a Greek word meaning change. It refers to the changes in all living organisms in order to maintain life, like growth, reproduction, etc.

subject: Biology
q: Energy is ______ during anabolism.
skill: Define metabolism, and differentiate between its types with examples
feedback: Anabolism includes biochemical reactions in which larger molecules are synthesised. This requires the use of energy.

subject: Biology
q: According to the lock and key hypothesis, what is represented by the lock and the key?
skill: Describe enzymes and explain the lock and key model and induced-fit model of enzyme action
feedback: The lock and key hypothesis was presented by German chemist Emil Fischer in order to explain the mechanism of enzyme action. According to this hypothesis, both enzyme and substrates possess specific shapes that fit exactly into one another. In other words, the enzyme is the lock, and the substrate is the key.

subject: Biology
q: The molecules that form as a result of an enzymatic reaction are called ______.
skill: Describe enzymes and explain the lock and key model and induced-fit model of enzyme action
feedback: Enzymes accelerate chemical reactions. The molecules upon which enzymes may act are called substrates. Enzymes convert substrates into different molecules known as products.

subject: Biology
q: Which of these can enter into the Krebs cycle?
skill: Describe enzymes and explain the lock and key model and induced-fit model of enzyme action
feedback: The Krebs cycle is a series of chemical reactions used by all aerobic organisms to release stored energy through the oxidation of acetyl-CoA derived from carbohydrates, fats, and proteins into carbon dioxide (CO\[_2\]) and chemical energy in the form of adenosine triphosphate (ATP).

subject: Biology
q: In the process of glycolysis, glucose is broken into _____.
skill: Describe enzymes and explain the lock and key model and induced-fit model of enzyme action
feedback: Glycolysis involves the breaking down of a sugar (generally glucose, although fructose and other sugars may be used) into more manageable compounds to produce energy. The end products of glycolysis are pyruvic acid+ ATP+ NADH.

subject: Biology
q: The primary role of cofactors is to ______.
skill: Explain how some enzymes require co-factors to function
feedback: Most enzymes do not function optimally, or even at all, unless they are bound to other non-protein helper molecules called cofactors. These cofactors assist in enzyme activity.

subject: Biology
q: A coenzyme is an organic factor _____.
skill: Explain how some enzymes require co-factors to function
feedback: A coenzyme is an organic factor loosely attached to an enzyme. Some enzymes require additional molecules such as coenzymes to function properly.

subject: Biology
q: An organic cofactor tightly bound to an enzyme is known as a _____.
skill: Explain how some enzymes require co-factors to function
feedback: A prosthetic group is an organic cofactor that tightly binds to enzymes. Cofactors can be both organic and inorganic. A coenzyme is organic, but it is loosely bound to enzymes.

subject: Biology
q: Cofactors are non-protein molecules or ions that _____.
options: can be organic or inorganic in nature
skill: Explain how some enzymes require co-factors to function
feedback: Cofactors are non-protein molecules or ions that can be organic or inorganic in nature. Some enzymes require additional molecules such as cofactors to function properly.

subject: Biology
q: Trypsin is an enzyme that will denature if the pH level is _____.
skill: Discuss the impact of pH, temperature, and concentration of substrate with enzyme activity
feedback: Trypsin is an enzyme that will start to denature if the pH level is anywhere below 7. The optimum temperature for trypsin is 8. At 6 it will denature.

subject: Biology
q: When all active sites of the enzymes are filled by substrates, and the remaining substrates cannot find free active sites, it is called _____ of active sites.
skill: Discuss the impact of pH, temperature, and concentration of substrate with enzyme activity
feedback: If the concentration of enzymes is kept constant and the amount of substrates is increased, a point will reach where there will be no effect on the rate of reaction. This is because all the active sites of the enzymes are filled by substrates. This is known as saturation.

subject: Biology
q: When we add more substrate to an already occurring enzymatic reaction and there is no increase in the rate of reaction, what would you predict?
options: All active sites are occupied by substrate molecules.
skill: Discuss the impact of pH, temperature, and concentration of substrate with enzyme activity
feedback: If the concentration of enzymes is kept constant and the amount of substrates is increased, a point will reach where there will be no effect on the rate of reaction. This is because all the active sites of the enzymes are filled by substrates.

subject: Biology
q: A battery is used to run a motor in order to lift a heavy weight. Which of the following is an accurate representation of this in the human body?
skill: Define bioenergetics, and describe oxidation-reduction reactions for the flow of energy
feedback: A battery provides electricity to the motor, which converts it into mechanical energy and uses it to pick up a heavy load. Similarly, food is the source of energy in our body. This energy then results in movement in our muscles.

subject: Biology
q: In which of the following processes, does oxidation and reduction of molecules <u>NOT</u> occur?
skill: Define bioenergetics, and describe oxidation-reduction reactions for the flow of energy
feedback: Photosynthesis, respiration, and digestion, all involve the exchange of electrons, and therefore involve oxidation and reduction reactions. Neutralisation is a chemical reaction between acids and bases to form salt and water. Neutralization does not involve any transfer of electrons and is therefore NOT a redox reaction.

subject: Biology
q: Name redox reaction(s) that take(s) place in plants.
skill: Define bioenergetics, and describe oxidation-reduction reactions for the flow of energy
feedback: Both photosynthesis and respiration involve the production and release of energy. Therefore, both are good examples of redox reactions that take place in plants. Transpiration is a process by which water is transported all over the plant.

subject: Biology
q: The study of energy relationships and transformations in living organisms is known as _____.
skill: Define bioenergetics, and describe oxidation-reduction reactions for the flow of energy
feedback: Bioenergetics is the study of energy relationships and transformations in living organisms.

subject: Biology
q: In living organisms, redox reactions involve the loss and gain of _____ atoms.
skill: Define bioenergetics, and describe oxidation-reduction reactions for the flow of energy
feedback: In living organisms, redox reactions involve the loss and gain of hydrogen atom, since we know each hydrogen atom contains one proton and one electron.

subject: Biology
q: Redox reactions involve the exchange of _____.
skill: Define bioenergetics, and describe oxidation-reduction reactions for the flow of energy
feedback: Redox reactions involve the exchange of electrons between atoms. The loss of electrons is called oxidation, while the gain of electrons is called reduction.

subject: Biology
q: When chemical bonds are broken down, a large amount of kinetic energy is usually released while the rest escapes as _____.
skill: Define bioenergetics, and describe oxidation-reduction reactions for the flow of energy
feedback: Food contains potential energy in its chemical bonds. When these bonds are broken down, a large amount of kinetic energy is usually released. Some of this energy is stored in the form of potential energy in the bonds of ATP molecules while the rest escapes as heat. The potential energy stored in ATP is again transformed into kinetic energy to carry out life activities.

subject: Biology
q: Potential energy is stored in chemical bonds and is released as _____ energy when these bonds break.
skill: Define bioenergetics, and describe oxidation-reduction reactions for the flow of energy
feedback: Potential energy is stored in the form of chemical bonds and is released as kinetic energy when these bonds break to carry out processes and movement in the body.

subject: Biology
q: There are _____ phosphate groups in an ADP molecule.
skill: Describe the role of adenosine triphosphate (ATP), and its synthesis through the ATP-ADP cycle
feedback: Adenosine diphosphate, also known as ADP, has two phosphate groups in it.

subject: Biology
q: Ribose is a _____ carbon sugar.
skill: Describe the role of adenosine triphosphate (ATP), and its synthesis through the ATP-ADP cycle
feedback: Ribose is a five-carbon sugar. Ribose is a simple sugar and carbohydrate with molecular formula \[C_5H_{10}O_5\]. It is a structural component of ATP, RNA, and other biomolecules.

subject: Biology
q: The major energy currency of all cells is _____.
skill: Describe the role of adenosine triphosphate (ATP), and its synthesis through the ATP-ADP cycle
feedback: Adenosine triphosphate, or ATP, is the primary energy currency in cells; ATP stores energy in its phosphate bonds.

subject: Biology
q: Which bonds of an ATP molecule, when broken down, release energy?
skill: Describe the role of adenosine triphosphate (ATP), and its synthesis through the ATP-ADP cycle
feedback: The breakdown of phosphate (P-P) bonds in an ATP molecule releases energy.

subject: Biology
q: The ATP molecule was discovered in ______.
skill: Describe the role of adenosine triphosphate (ATP), and its synthesis through the ATP-ADP cycle
feedback: ATP was discovered in 1929 by Karl Lohmann and was proposed to be the main energy-transfer molecule in the cell by the Nobel prize-winner Fritz Lipmann in 1941.

subject: Biology
q: Which bonds of ATP molecules produce energy?
skill: Describe the role of adenosine triphosphate (ATP), and its synthesis through the ATP-ADP cycle
feedback: The bond between two phosphate groups is a phospho-anhydride bond. This bond is less stable and is considered a high-energy bond. Adenosine diphosphate (ADP) can provide more energy than AMP. In ATP, there are three phosphate groups with two high-energy bonds.

subject: Biology
q: ATP was discovered by
skill: Describe the role of adenosine triphosphate (ATP), and its synthesis through the ATP-ADP cycle
feedback: ATP was discovered in in 1929 by Karl Lohmann and was proposed to be the main energy transfer molecule in the cell by the Nobel prize-winner Fritz Lipmann in 1941.

subject: Biology
q: Why will the leaves of a rainforest plant turn yellow if it is placed in a desert?
options: The plant will receive too many nutrients from the desert soil.
The plant will not receive enough carbon dioxide in the desert.
The plant will not receive enough water in the desert.
The plant will not receive enough oxygen in the desert.
skill: Describe photosynthesis and discuss its importance
feedback:
Deserts have dry conditions, so the plant will not receive enough water in the desert. Due to the lack of water, the leaves of a plant may turn yellow.

Certain plant species can live in the desert because they are adapted to survive with a small amount of water. However, a plant that was originally in a rainforest will not have any such adaptations.

subject: Biology
q:
The equation below shows the chemical reaction responsible for photosynthesis. Which reactant is missing?

_____ + water → glucose + oxygen
skill: Describe photosynthesis and discuss its importance
feedback: In photosynthesis, carbon dioxide and water react in the presence of sunlight to form glucose and oxygen. This reaction takes place in the leaves of plants.

subject: Biology
q: What does the word <u>photo</u> mean in photosynthesis?
skill: Describe photosynthesis and discuss its importance
feedback: In photosynthesis, plants use sunlight to create glucose and oxygen from carbon dioxide and water. The word photo is originally a Greek word that means light.

subject: Biology
q: The flow chart showing the light reactions of photosynthesis is known as ______.
skill: Describe photosynthesis and discuss its importance
feedback: The whole series of light reaction is called the Z-scheme due to its Z-shaped flow chart.

subject: Biology
q: Oxidation and reduction
options: do NOT occur in photosynthesis or respiration.
skill: Describe photosynthesis and discuss its importance
feedback:
All living organisms must find a way to harvest energy in order to maintain life. Animals use cellular respiration in order to convert food into chemical energy. Plants release chemical energy through photosynthesis.
Redox reactions are an important process for cellular respiration and photosynthesis. These reactions are also known as oxidation-reduction. In a redox reaction, one reactant partially or completely transfers one or more electrons to another. The electron-losing reactant is known as the reducing agent and is considered oxidised in the reaction. The substance gaining electrons is referred to as the oxidising agent and is considered reduced.

subject: Biology
q: In the light reaction of photosynthesis, when electrons are passed through an electron transport chain, _____ is produced.
skill: Describe photosynthesis and discuss its importance
feedback: In the light reaction of photosynthesis, when chlorophyll molecules absorb light, their energy level increases and electrons are emitted. These electrons are then passed to the electron transport chain to produce ATP (adenosinetriphosphate).

subject: Biology
q: Which gas is a by-product of photosynthesis?
skill: Describe photosynthesis and discuss its importance
feedback: Photosynthesis is the synthesis of glucose from CO\[_2\] and H\[_2\]O in the presence of sunlight and chlorophyll, with oxygen as a by-product.

subject: Biology
q: In chloroplasts, the Calvin cycle takes place in _____.
skill: Describe photosynthesis and discuss its importance
feedback: The light-independent reactions, or dark reactions, of photosynthesis are chemical reactions that convert carbon dioxide and other compounds into glucose. These reactions occur in the stroma, the fluid-filled area of a chloroplast outside the thylakoid membranes.

subject: Biology
q: The diagram above shows the internal structure of a leaf. Which labelled part does NOT have any chloroplasts?
skill: Explain how the structure of a leaf is adapted for photosynthesis
feedback: The part labelled as A is an epidermal cell. Epidermal cells protect the layers below. They do not have a role in photosynthesis and do not contain any chloroplasts.

subject: Biology
q: Which cells do both plants and humans have?
skill: Explain how the structure of a leaf is adapted for photosynthesis
feedback: The surfaces of the bodies of both plants and humans are covered with epidermal cells. These cells protect the internal structure of the body.

subject: Biology
q: Palisade mesophyll cells in leaves are tightly packed while spongy mesophyll cells are loosely packed. How does this support photosynthesis?
options: The tightly packed palisade mesophyll cells protect the spongy mesophyll cells below them from excessive sunlight.
Photosynthesis occurs at a higher rate in spongy mesophyll cells as they have more chloroplasts.
The tightly packed mesophyll cells near the upper surface ensure that no gases can escape the leaf.
Carbon dioxide and water vapour can move in the empty spaces between spongy mesophyll cells.
skill: Explain how the structure of a leaf is adapted for photosynthesis
feedback: Carbon dioxide and water vapour are essential inputs in the process of photosynthesis. Once they enter the leaf, they need to reach the chloroplasts where photosynthesis takes place. They reach these chloroplasts by moving in the empty spaces between spongy mesophyll cells.

subject: Biology
q: Xylem vessels in plants carry water from _____ to leaves.
skill: Explain how the structure of a leaf is adapted for photosynthesis
feedback: Water is an essential input in photosynthesis. The roots of plants absorb water from the soil, and thin tube-like structures known as xylem vessels carry the water to the leaves.

subject: Biology
q: When it rains, water droplets that fall on leaves
options: slide off the leaves and fall to the ground.
are taken to the roots by xylem vessels.
skill: Explain how the structure of a leaf is adapted for photosynthesis
feedback: Excessive water absorption can be harmful to leaves. Therefore, they are covered by a waterproof, waxy layer known as a cuticle. The cuticle prevents water drops from entering the leaf, so they slide off and fall to the ground.

subject: Biology
q: Which statement about leaves is NOT true?
options: All leaves are adapted to support photosynthesis.
All leaves are broad and flat.
All leaves have empty spaces between cells.
skill: Explain how the structure of a leaf is adapted for photosynthesis
feedback: Several, but NOT all, leaves are broad and flat. Some water plants have leaves that are long and thin so they can reach the surface of the water. The specific shape of a plant species depends on the environment it grows in.

subject: Biology
q: A cell is taken from the upper surface of a leaf. It has numerous chloroplasts. It is likely to be
skill: Explain how the structure of a leaf is adapted for photosynthesis
feedback: Cells near the upper surface of a leaf are either epidermal cells or palisade mesophyll cells. Of these two cell types, palisade mesophyll cells contain chloroplasts. Spongy mesophyll cells also have chloroplasts but they are not near the upper surface.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following does not occur due to the accumulation of lactic acid in humans?
skill: Discuss the importance of anaerobic respiration, and the fate of lactic acid in the body
feedback: The production of lactic acid in our body can result in fatigue, muscular pain, or cramps. It does not, however, result in liver damage.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following statements comparing aerobic and anaerobic respiration is correct?
options: Aerobic respiration releases a smaller amount of energy than anaerobic respiration.
Anaerobic respiration only occurs in microbes while aerobic respiration only occurs in multicellular organisms.
Aerobic respiration is the complete breakdown of glucose while anaerobic respiration is the incomplete breakdown of glucose.
Anaerobic respiration does not produce carbon dioxide while aerobic respiration does.
skill: Discuss the importance of anaerobic respiration, and the fate of lactic acid in the body
feedback: Aerobic respiration takes place in the presence of oxygen, while anaerobic respiration takes place in the absence of oxygen. Since oxygen is essential for the conversion of glucose to energy, aerobic respiration is the complete breakdown of glucose while anaerobic respiration is the incomplete breakdown of glucose.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following organs is involved in the oxidation of lactic acid?
skill: Discuss the importance of anaerobic respiration, and the fate of lactic acid in the body
feedback: The liver is responsible for the oxidation of lactic acid.

subject: Biology
q: The graph below shows the concentration of lactic acid in the blood against time. The time is divided into three sections, P, Q, and R. Based on the graph, which of the following statements is true?
options: P represents the time during intense physical exertion.
R represent the time after intense physical exertion.
Q represents the time after intense physical exertion.
R represents the time during intense physical exertion.
skill: Discuss the importance of anaerobic respiration, and the fate of lactic acid in the body
feedback: Anaerobic respiration takes place when there is not enough oxygen. As a result, lactic acid is produced. This occurs at a faster rate because the body needs energy. However, when the body is at rest, the oxygen debt needs to be replenished. This takes time, therefore, the production of lactic acid ends up being more rapid than its removal.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following processes produces lactic acid?
skill: Discuss the importance of anaerobic respiration, and the fate of lactic acid in the body
feedback: Fermentation produces ethanol. Lactation is the production of milk. Photosynthesis is the production of glucose. Anaerobic respiration produces lactic acid.

subject: Biology
q: An oxygen debt occurs when _____.
options: the level of energy produced exceeds the level of energy demanded
gaseous exchange cannot keep up with the oxygen requirement
there is excess carbon dioxide in the body
the rate of photosynthesis is lower than the rate of respiration
skill: Discuss the importance of anaerobic respiration, and the fate of lactic acid in the body
feedback: An oxygen debt is when gaseous exchanges cannot keep up with the oxygen requirements within the body.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following statements about lactic acid is incorrect?
options: After exercise, lactic acid is removed from the muscles and brought to the liver.
Anaerobic respiration always produces lactic acid.
An oxygen debt is incurred when lactic acid accumulates in the muscles.
The build-up of lactic acid in the muscles results in fatigue.
skill: Discuss the importance of anaerobic respiration, and the fate of lactic acid in the body
feedback: Anaerobic respiration is a form of respiration that takes place in the absence of oxygen. Glucose is not completely oxidised and so it forms ethanol or lactic acid. It does not always produce lactic acid.

subject: Biology
q: Oxygen is needed for _____.
options: Krebs cycle and electron transport chain
skill: State how respiratory energy is obtained and used in the body
feedback: Glycolysis can take place without oxygen. However, the Krebs cycle and electron transport chain can't. Both processes need oxygen.

subject: Biology
q: In which of the following steps of respiration, is CO<sub>2</sub> produced?
skill: State how respiratory energy is obtained and used in the body
feedback: In one turn of the Krebs cycle, 2 molecules of CO2 are produced.

subject: Biology
q: At the end of the respiratory electron transport chain, what is used to form H<sub>2</sub>O?
skill: State how respiratory energy is obtained and used in the body
feedback: At the end of a respiratory electron transport chain, oxygen picks up protons and electrons to form H2O.

subject: Biology
q: Before entering the Krebs cycle, pyruvic acid is converted into _____.
skill: State how respiratory energy is obtained and used in the body
feedback: Pyruvic acid is a three-carbon molecule that reacts with coenzyme A, and forms acetyl-CoA.

subject: Biology
q: Glycolysis, Krebs cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation takes place in the _____, _____, and _____ respectively.
skill: State how respiratory energy is obtained and used in the body
feedback: Cellular respiration is a three-step process. Glycolysis is the first step and takes place in the cytoplasm. Both the Krebs cycle and oxidative phosphorylation (electron transport chain) take place in the mitochondria.

subject: Biology
q: Each NADH generated in glycolysis is equal to _____.
skill: State how respiratory energy is obtained and used in the body
feedback: NAD+ is a coenzyme that is reduced to NADH. Each NADH product generates 3 ATP molecules.

subject: Biology
q: The amount of ATP produced from one molecule of glucose during aerobic respiration is _____.
skill: State how respiratory energy is obtained and used in the body
feedback: The amount of ATP produced from one molecule of glucose during aerobic respiration is 36-38.

subject: Biology
q: By the end of glycolysis, _____ molecules are produced.
skill: State how respiratory energy is obtained and used in the body
feedback: Glycolysis takes place in the cytoplasm and leads to the production of 6 ATP. However, 2 ATP molecules are used to transfer the molecules across the mitochondria to the next step. Therefore, by the end of glycolysis, 4 ATP molecules are produced.

subject: Biology
q: In a graph that measures the rate of photosynthesis, the y-axis shows the increase in the mass of a plant. What should the x-axis show?
options: the number of oxygen bubbles produced
skill: Explain the concept of limiting factors in photosynthesis
feedback: The rate of photosynthesis describes how fast photosynthesis is happening in a given time. To measure this, the time that has lapsed while photosynthesis is occurring should be tracked. Therefore, in the graph, the x-axis should show time.

subject: Biology
q: Why are water plants commonly used in experiments to measure the rate of photosynthesis?
options: Changing the water level makes it easier to see the effect of water on the rate of photosynthesis.
The oxygen bubbles produced by water plants make it easier to measure the rate of photosynthesis.
The carbon dioxide bubbles produced by water plants make it easier to measure the rate of photosynthesis.
The temperature of the water can easily be adjusted to see if the rate of photosynthesis is affected.
skill: Explain the concept of limiting factors in photosynthesis
feedback: Measuring the rate of photosynthesis accurately in a plant is not easy. One method is to measure the amount of oxygen that is produced from photosynthesis. Oxygen production can easily be tracked in water plants by counting the oxygen bubbles produced.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following statements about photosynthesis is NOT true?
options: Plants need to be watered continuously to increase the rate of photosynthesis.
The ideal percentage of carbon dioxide needed for photosynthesis is greater than 0.04%
The amount of heat a plant receives can affect its rate of photosynthesis.
Plants produce sugar at a faster rate when the conditions for photosynthesis are ideal.
skill: Explain the concept of limiting factors in photosynthesis
feedback: While water is essential for photosynthesis, an excess of water can be harmful to plants. Excess water can reduce air spaces in the soil and affect the balance of fluids in cells. Therefore, plants DO NOT need to be watered continuously to increase the rate of photosynthesis.

subject: Biology
q:
Which of the following factors affect the rate of photosynthesis?

I. temperature
II. sugar
III. sunlight
IV. water
skill: Explain the concept of limiting factors in photosynthesis
feedback: Water and sunlight are essential for photosynthesis. Temperature also affects the rate of photosynthesis. However, sugar is a product of photosynthesis, not an input.

subject: Biology
q:
Choose the benefit(s) that can result from keeping plants in a greenhouse.

I. The green colour of the walls in a greenhouse is ideal for photosynthesis.
II. The temperature of the greenhouse can be controlled.
III. The plants are protected from extreme temperatures.
skill: Explain the concept of limiting factors in photosynthesis
feedback: Greenhouses are made of clear glass or plastic, not a green wall. If the walls were green, sunlight would not be able to enter. In greenhouses, the temperature of the greenhouse can be controlled, and the plants are protected from extreme temperatures. Temperature is important as it can affect the rate of photosynthesis.

subject: Biology
q: What is the percentage of oxygen in the atmosphere?
skill: Explain the concept of limiting factors in photosynthesis
feedback: 21% of the air in the atmosphere is composed of oxygen. This percentage is kept at a stable rate because plants create oxygen through the process of photosynthesis. This oxygen enters the environment after being produced by plants.

subject: Biology
q: Sana thinks that the rate of photosynthesis is not affected by temperature. Her thinking is an example of
skill: Explain the concept of limiting factors in photosynthesis
feedback: The rate of photosynthesis is affected by temperature, so Sana's thinking or belief is incorrect. Such a belief is known as a hypothesis. It can be proven correct or incorrect only by carrying out an experiment.

subject: Biology
q: The diagram above shows the internal structure of a leaf. Which labelled part does NOT have any chloroplasts?
skill: Compare photosynthesis and respiration in plants
feedback: The part labelled as A is an epidermal cell. Epidermal cells protect the layers below. They do not have a role in photosynthesis and do not contain any chloroplasts.

subject: Biology
q: Which cells do both plants and humans have?
skill: Compare photosynthesis and respiration in plants
feedback: The surfaces of the bodies of both plants and humans are covered with epidermal cells. These cells protect the internal structure of the body.

subject: Biology
q: Palisade mesophyll cells in leaves are tightly packed while spongy mesophyll cells are loosely packed. How does this support photosynthesis?
options: The tightly packed palisade mesophyll cells protect the spongy mesophyll cells below them from excessive sunlight.
Photosynthesis occurs at a higher rate in spongy mesophyll cells as they have more chloroplasts.
The tightly packed mesophyll cells near the upper surface ensure that no gases can escape the leaf.
Carbon dioxide and water vapour can move in the empty spaces between spongy mesophyll cells.
skill: Compare photosynthesis and respiration in plants
feedback: Carbon dioxide and water vapour are essential inputs in the process of photosynthesis. Once they enter the leaf, they need to reach the chloroplasts where photosynthesis takes place. They reach these chloroplasts by moving in the empty spaces between spongy mesophyll cells.

subject: Biology
q: Xylem vessels in plants carry water from _____ to leaves.
skill: Compare photosynthesis and respiration in plants
feedback: Water is an essential input in photosynthesis. The roots of plants absorb water from the soil, and thin tube-like structures known as xylem vessels carry the water to the leaves.

subject: Biology
q: When it rains, water droplets that fall on leaves
options: slide off the leaves and fall to the ground.
are taken to the roots by xylem vessels.
skill: Compare photosynthesis and respiration in plants
feedback: Excessive water absorption can be harmful to leaves. Therefore, they are covered by a waterproof, waxy layer known as a cuticle. The cuticle prevents water drops from entering the leaf, so they slide off and fall to the ground.

subject: Biology
q: Which statement about leaves is NOT true?
options: All leaves are adapted to support photosynthesis.
All leaves are broad and flat.
All leaves have empty spaces between cells.
skill: Compare photosynthesis and respiration in plants
feedback: Several, but NOT all, leaves are broad and flat. Some water plants have leaves that are long and thin so they can reach the surface of the water. The specific shape of a plant species depends on the environment it grows in.

subject: Biology
q: A cell is taken from the upper surface of a leaf. It has numerous chloroplasts. It is likely to be
skill: Compare photosynthesis and respiration in plants
feedback: Cells near the upper surface of a leaf are either epidermal cells or palisade mesophyll cells. Of these two cell types, palisade mesophyll cells contain chloroplasts. Spongy mesophyll cells also have chloroplasts but they are not near the upper surface.

subject: Biology
q: The gaseous exchange in a leaf mostly occurs through _____.
skill: Compare photosynthesis and respiration in plants
feedback: The leaves and young stems have stomata in their epidermis. The gaseous exchange occurs through these stomata. The inner cells of leaves (mesophyll) and stems also have air spaces among them , which help in the exchange of gases.

subject: Biology
q: All the following are macro minerals, except _____.
skill: Classify minerals into macro and micro nutrients, and their importance to plants
feedback: Potassium, calcium, and magnesium are macronutrients. They are nutrients that needed in large amounts to support plant growth. Chromium, on the other hand, is not an essential nutrient.

subject: Biology
q: Plants can be grown in _____.
options: the soil only, without essential nutrients
either water or the soil with essential nutrients
either water or the soil, but without essential nutrients
skill: Classify minerals into macro and micro nutrients, and their importance to plants
feedback: Plants can grow in either water or soil with essential nutrients.

subject: Biology
q: Which one of the following roles is not characteristic of an essential element?
options: changing the chemistry of the soil
being a structural component of energy-related chemical compounds
skill: Classify minerals into macro and micro nutrients, and their importance to plants
feedback: Essential elements are added to the soil to be absorbed by the plant to help and support it in its processes. They do not change the chemistry of the soil.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following is a macronutrient for plants?
skill: Classify minerals into macro and micro nutrients, and their importance to plants
feedback: Nutrients that are required in massive quantities are called macronutrients. From the options above, sulphur is a macronutrient, while the others are micronutrients.

subject: Biology
q: Micronutrients of plants are _____.
options: available in the soil in large amounts
required by plants in small amounts
useful but not required by plants
skill: Classify minerals into macro and micro nutrients, and their importance to plants
feedback:
Plants require nutrients which are essential for successful growth and optimum yields. Without their presence, the consequences can range from stunted growth, leaf discoloration, and loss of fruiting bodies, all of which lead to reduced crop yields.

Essential nutrients can be divided into macronutrients and micronutrients. Nutrients that plants require in larger amounts are called macronutrients and those required in small amounts are known as micronutrients.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following occurs when a plant absorbs too much magnesium?
skill: Discuss the importance of nitrogen and magnesium on plant growth
feedback: Too much magnesium in the soil can affect a plant's ability to obtain other vital minerals, such as, calcium. This results in a calcium deficiency which causes plants to lose their green colour and turn brown or yellow.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following occurs when a plant absorbs too much nitrogen?
skill: Discuss the importance of nitrogen and magnesium on plant growth
feedback: When a plant absorbs too much nitrogen, it starts to grow rapidly and uncontrollably. This can delay other important development processes in plants, such as fruiting and flowering.

subject: Biology
q: Which mineral deficiency could the plant shown below be suffering from?
skill: Discuss the importance of nitrogen and magnesium on plant growth
feedback: Magnesium and nitrogen are parts of the chlorophyll molecule which gives the plant its green colour. Since both magnesium and nitrogen are used to make chlorophyll, lack of either mineral will cause the colour of the plant to turn yellow and show poor growth.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following elements are required to produce chlorophyll?
skill: Discuss the importance of nitrogen and magnesium on plant growth
feedback: Both magnesium and nitrogen are necessary for making chlorophyll molecules and are thus required to produce chlorophyll.

subject: Biology
q: Plants primarily obtain nitrogen in the form of _____.
skill: Discuss the importance of nitrogen and magnesium on plant growth
feedback: Plants do not get their nitrogen directly from the air. Atmospheric nitrogen first diffuses into the soil where it is converted into nitrates and other nitrogen compounds by microorganisms in the soil. These nitrogen compounds are easier for plants to use. Most of the plant's nitrogen is obtained in the form of these nitrates.

subject: Biology
q: Eutrophication is primarily caused by _____.
skill: Describe the advantages and disadvantages of fertilisers
feedback: Eutrophication is the increase in the concentration of essential plant nutrients such as phosphorous, nitrogen, etc. This condition is primarily caused by excessive use of inorganic fertiliser since they are more soluble in water as compared to organic fertilisers and are washed away by rain into rivers and lakes. The nutrients in the fertilisers then cause eutrophication.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following is NOT an advantage of inorganic fertilisers as compared to organic fertilisers?
options: They are absorbed more quickly by the plant.
They enhance the ability of soil to hold nutrients.
They are more soluble in water.
skill: Describe the advantages and disadvantages of fertilisers
feedback: Organic fertilisers are derived from animal and plant materials that take time to be broken down into forms that can easily be used by the plant.  Their natural fertilisers improve the soil's texture, enabling it to hold moisture and nutrients better. Inorganic fertilisers are mostly in the form of beads or powders and do not improve the texture of the soil. However, inorganic fertilisers usually have a higher mineral content, higher solubility, and are quickly absorbed by the plants as compared to organic fertilisers.

subject: Biology
q: Which of these fertilisers is NOT an inorganic fertiliser?
skill: Describe the advantages and disadvantages of fertilisers
feedback: Synthetically made fertilisers or naturally occurring inorganic compounds are classified as inorganic fertilisers. On the other hand, fertilisers that are derived from decaying living things are classified as organic fertilisers. Compost is basically decomposed organic material made from leaves, twigs, manure, scraps from plants etc. Therefore, it is NOT an inorganic fertiliser.

subject: Biology
q: Which of these is NOT an advantage of using fertilisers?
skill: Describe the advantages and disadvantages of fertilisers
feedback: Fertilisers increase the amount of nutrients available in the soil, which increases the yield and growth rate of plants. Eutrophication is the process by which over usage of fertilisers causes the nutrients to wash away into rivers and lakes. This results in overgrowth of algae on the water and may cause oxygen depletion as the algae are decomposed by bacteria. Eutrophication is a serious environmental hazard to aquatic life, and therefore, is NOT an advantage of fertilisers.

subject: Biology
q: Fertilisers are primarily classified as _____ and _____.
skill: Describe the advantages and disadvantages of fertilisers
feedback: There are many ways of classifying fertilisers. The most useful way is to broadly divide them into organic and inorganic fertilisers. Organic fertilisers only contain plant or animal-based materials while inorganic fertilisers consist of minerals and synthetic chemicals.

subject: Biology
q: _____ are the main source of energy for animals.
skill: Differentiate among carbohydrates, proteins and fats
feedback: Carbohydrates are the main source of energy for animals. More than half of the energy used by animals is provided by carbohydrates.

subject: Biology
q: Lipids are used to form _____.
skill: Differentiate among carbohydrates, proteins and fats
feedback: Lipids are used to form cell membranes. Extra glucose is stored in the form of starch in plants, and in the form of glycogen in animals. Nucleic acids are the genetic material of cells. The two main classes or types of nucleic acids are deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) and ribonucleic acid (RNA).

subject: Biology
q: Which biomolecule produces the most energy per gram?
skill: Differentiate among carbohydrates, proteins and fats
feedback:
Lipids produce the most energy per gram. One gram of lipids produces 9 kilocalories of energy.
Both carbohydrates and proteins produce only 4 kilocalories of energy per gram. Minerals are not used as energy sources.

subject: Biology
q: Which group of enzymes breaks up starches and other carbohydrates?
skill: Differentiate among carbohydrates, proteins and fats
feedback:
An amylase is an enzyme that catalyses the hydrolysis of starch into sugars. It is present in the saliva of humans and other mammals, where it begins the chemical process of digestion.
However, proteases and lipases are involved in breakdown of proteins and fats.

subject: Biology
q: What is the primary nutrient that provides quick useable energy for the body?
skill: Differentiate among carbohydrates, proteins and fats
feedback:
Carbohydrates are the basic source of energy that provide quick useable energy for the body. About half to \[2/3\] of the total calories humans consume daily are from carbohydrates.

Glucose is the most often used carbohydrate for energy. On the other hand, protein, nucleic acid, and lipids are vital building component for body. They can be used as a source of energy but not able to provide quick useable form of energy for the body

subject: Biology
q: Why is whole wheat bread considered to be healthier than white bread?
options: Whole wheat bread has more calories.
Whole wheat bread has more carbohydrates.
Whole wheat bread has more fibre.
Whole wheat bread has more minerals.
skill: Describe the concept and need for a balanced diet, relating it to age, gender and activity
feedback: Whole wheat bread is healthier than white bread because it contains more fibre. Fibre relieves constipation and lowers cholesterol and blood sugar.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following foods has the highest carbohydrate percentage?
skill: Describe the concept and need for a balanced diet, relating it to age, gender and activity
feedback: Eggs and chicken mainly consist of proteins and lipids and are almost completely devoid of carbohydrates. Both rice and bread are high carb foods, but bread is 52% carbs while rice is 23%. Therefore, bread is the correct answer.

subject: Biology
q: Milk does NOT contain which of the following nutrients?
skill: Describe the concept and need for a balanced diet, relating it to age, gender and activity
feedback: Milk is primarily composed of water and is 3% proteins and 4% carbohydrates and 4% lipids. Thus, none of these nutrients are absent in milk.

subject: Biology
q: Which of these requires the most protein/kg of body weight?
skill: Describe the concept and need for a balanced diet, relating it to age, gender and activity
feedback: Proteins are required by the body for the growth of tissue. Children require the most protein/kg of body weight as they are still in the growing phase. It also helps repair cells, supply energy, and is a source of varying amounts of vitamins.

subject: Biology
q: The percentage of proteins in a whole chicken is _____.
skill: Describe the concept and need for a balanced diet, relating it to age, gender and activity
feedback: The percentage of protein in a whole chicken is \[20\% \]

subject: Biology
q: The digestive process starts in the _____.
skill: Describe the importance of digestion in terms of absorption and assimilation of food
feedback: The digestive process starts in the mouth. The salivary glands in the mouth act on the food and start the digestion of starch into sugars.

subject: Biology
q:
Which of the following organs is/are a part of the alimentary canal?
  • the stomach
  • the liver
  • the small intestine
skill: Describe the importance of digestion in terms of absorption and assimilation of food
feedback:
Only the stomach and the small intestine are parts of the alimentary canal.
The liver is a digestive gland. It is not a part of the alimentary canal because it is not in direct contact with food. It secretes bile into the small intestine which helps in the digestion process.

subject: Biology
q:
Which of the following statements is/are true about the digestive system?

  1. The organs of the digestive system are divided into two groups.
  2. There are three types of salivary glands in the mouth.
  3. The liver, pancreas, gastric glands, and salivary glands are not part of the alimentary canal.
skill: Describe the importance of digestion in terms of absorption and assimilation of food
feedback:
The organs of the digestive system are divided into two groups: alimentary canal and the digestive glands. So statement I is true.
There are three pairs of salivary glands in the mouth which secrete saliva. So, statement II is also true.
The liver, pancreas, gastric glands, and salivary glands are the digestive glands of the digestive system. They are not parts of the alimentary canal. So, statement III is true as well.

subject: Biology
q: Bile is secreted by the _____ and stored in the _____.
skill: Describe the importance of digestion in terms of absorption and assimilation of food
feedback: Bile is the yellowish-green liquid secreted by the liver and is stored in the gall bladder. Inside the gall bladder, the bile becomes concentrated and is then secreted into the small intestine during the process of digestion.

subject: Biology
q: The digestive system consists of a _____
options: group of organs that works together to transport oxygen and blood throughout the body.
group of organs that works together to take in oxygen and expel carbon dioxide from the body.
group of organs that works together to break down food and provide energy to the body.
group of organs that works together to remove waste from the body.
skill: Describe the importance of digestion in terms of absorption and assimilation of food
feedback:
The group of organs that work together to break down food and provide energy to the body is called the digestive system.
The group of organs that work together to transport oxygen and blood all over the body is known as the circulatory system.
The group of organs that work together to take in oxygen and expel carbon dioxide from the body is known as the respiratory system.
The group of organs that work together to remove waste from the body is known as the excretory system.

subject: Biology
q: Calcium along with low salt intake and high potassium intake prevents _____.
skill: Identify food sources and metabolic functions of vitamins A, C, D and K and minerals like calcium and iron
feedback: A high salt intake can result in high blood pressure. If not looked after, hypertension can lead to a heart attack. Therefore, a good calcium, high potassium, and low salt diet can prevent hypertension.

subject: Biology
q: Why are calcium and iron needed by the body?
options: They are both needed to strengthen bones.
They are both needed to produce blood.
Calcium is needed for the blood, while iron is needed for the bones.
Calcium is needed for the bones, while iron is needed for the blood.
skill: Identify food sources and metabolic functions of vitamins A, C, D and K and minerals like calcium and iron
feedback: Calcium is an important component for bones, whereas iron is an important component of blood. Children need both in healthy quantities to grow.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following is a water-soluble vitamin?
skill: Identify food sources and metabolic functions of vitamins A, C, D and K and minerals like calcium and iron
feedback: Water-soluble vitamins dissolve in water and are not stored by the body. Since they are eliminated in urine, we require a continuous daily supply in our diet. Water-soluble vitamins include vitamin B and vitamin C.

subject: Biology
q: Which vitamin causes a loss of appetite and liver problems when taken in excess?
skill: Identify food sources and metabolic functions of vitamins A, C, D and K and minerals like calcium and iron
feedback: An excess amount of vitamin A is stored in one's liver and accumulates over time. It may cause a loss of appetite as well.

subject: Biology
q: Severe dehydration may cause _____.
skill: Identify sources and functions of water and dietary fibre in the body, and the effects of their deficiencies
feedback: Severe dehydration may cause cardiovascular problems . Prolonged dehydration can thicken the blood due to the lack of water. Thick blood flows very slowly through the blood vessels, and in extreme cases, may cause other cardiovascular problems, such as a heart attack.

subject: Biology
q: A human adult requires roughly _____ of water every day.
skill: Identify sources and functions of water and dietary fibre in the body, and the effects of their deficiencies
feedback: A human adult requires roughly 2 litres of water every day. This amount varies depending on the gender, weight, and age of a person. Other sources of water intake are milk, juicy fruits, and vegetables.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following helps in regulating body temperature?
skill: Identify sources and functions of water and dietary fibre in the body, and the effects of their deficiencies
feedback: One of the many essential functions of water is to maintain regular body temperature through sweating. Whensweat evaporates off the skin, it creates a cooling effect, which helps in regulating body temperature.

subject: Biology
q: Approximately _____ of the human body is composed of water.
skill: Identify sources and functions of water and dietary fibre in the body, and the effects of their deficiencies
feedback: Approximately 60% of the human body is composed of water. Nearly all life-sustaining chemical reactions require an aqueous (watery) environment. The average human adult male body comprises 60-63% of water, while the average adult female body is made up of 52-55% of water. These values vary greatly depending on factors such as the weight, age, and height of individuals.

subject: Biology
q: A skeletal appearance in children aged between 6 months and one-year is known as _____.
skill: Describe the deficiency diseases of vitamins A, C, D and K and of calcium and iron
feedback: Marasmus is a form of severe malnutrition characterised by energy deficiency. It can be distinguished from kwashiorkor in that kwashiorkor is protein deficiency with adequate energy intake whereas marasmus is inadequate energy intake in all forms, including protein.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following is a symptom of vitamin A deficiency?
skill: Describe the deficiency diseases of vitamins A, C, D and K and of calcium and iron
feedback: Night blindness is a deficiency of vitamin A.

subject: Biology
q: In children, vitamin D deficiency causes a condition known as _____.
skill: Describe the deficiency diseases of vitamins A, C, D and K and of calcium and iron
feedback: Vitamin D deficiency can lead to a loss of bone density, and severe vitamin D deficiency can also lead to other diseases. In children, it can cause rickets. Rickets is a rare disease that causes the bones to become soft and bend.

subject: Biology
q: Vitamin C deficiency can result in a condition called _____.
skill: Describe the deficiency diseases of vitamins A, C, D and K and of calcium and iron
feedback: Symptoms of scurvy result from a severe vitamin C deficiency. They include bleeding sores, tooth loss, anaemia, and a reduced rate of healing for injuries.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following is NOT a major symptom of protein malnutrition?
skill: Describe the problems of protein energy malnutrition and over-intake of nutrients
feedback: Proteins are used to produce cells and tissues; therefore, a deficiency of protein affects nearly all body functions. The major symptoms of protein deficiency, however, are weakened immunity, stunted growth, and declining mental function. Sometimes, changes in skin pigment can be observed resulting in hyperpigmentation Hence, hyperpigmentation is not a major symptom of protein malnutrition.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following statements best describes malnutrition?
skill: Describe the problems of protein energy malnutrition and over-intake of nutrients
feedback: Malnutrition is a broad term that covers eating too less and too much. It also covers having too little of a certain type of food which may result in a deficiency. Therefore, malnutrition can be best described as not taking enough nutrients.

subject: Biology
q: Protein malnutrition can lead to which of the following diseases?
skill: Describe the problems of protein energy malnutrition and over-intake of nutrients
feedback: kwashiorkor is a protein deficiency caused at the age of about 12 months when breast milk is discontinued. Children suffering from this disease may have a normal height but are abnormally thin.

subject: Biology
q:
Which of the following are secreted into the small intestine?

I.   intestinal juice
II.  pancreatic juice
III. bile
skill: Describe the structure and function of the alimentary canal
feedback:
Intestinal juice, pancreatic juice, and bile are all secreted into the small intestine.
Bile is produced by the liver, pancreatic juice is produced by the pancreas, and intestinal juice is produced by the small intestine.

subject: Biology
q: The semifluid, semisolid form of food, which passes from the stomach to the small intestine is called _____.
skill: Describe the structure and function of the alimentary canal
feedback: After the food is digested in the stomach for a few hours, it is partly digested into an acidic, semi-solid form called the chyme. The chyme then enters the small intestine.

subject: Biology
q: The process of breaking down complex food into smaller substances so it can be absorbed by the bloodstream is carried out by the _____.
skill: Describe the structure and function of the alimentary canal
feedback: The process of breaking down food into smaller, absorbable substances is called digestion and it is carried out by a group of organs called the digestive system.

subject: Biology
q: The hepatic portal vein carries blood from the _____ to the _____.
skill: Describe the structure and function of the alimentary canal
feedback: In the small intestine, each villus is richly supplied with blood capillaries and a vessel of the lymphatic system, called lacteal. The walls of a villus have the thickness of a single cell. The digested molecules such as simple sugars and amino acids are absorbed from the intestine into the blood capillaries present in villi. Blood carries them away from the small intestine via the hepatic portal vein and goes to the liver for filtering. Here, toxins are removed, and extra food is stored.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following does NOT occur in the oral cavity?
options: breaking the food into small fragments
breakdown of glucose to release energy
skill: Describe the structure and function of the alimentary canal
feedback:
The essential functions of oral cavity are:

1. Food selection: When food enters oral cavity, it is tasted and felt.
2. Grinding of food: Food is broken into small fragments to begin the process of digestion.
3. Lubrication and chemical digestion of food: The chewing process stimulates the three pairs of salivary glands (under tongue, behind jaws, and in front of ears) to release a juice called saliva in oral cavity.

Glucose is NOT broken down to release energy in the oral cavity.

subject: Biology
q: The pancreas releases digestive enzymes into the _____.
skill: Describe the structure and function of the alimentary canal
feedback:
The duodenum compromises of the first 10 inches (25cm) of small intestine and it is the part of the alimentary canal where most of the digestive process occurs. Here, food is further mixed with 3 different secretions:
1. Bile from the liver helps in the digestion of lipids through emulsification.
2. Pancreatic juice from the pancreas contains the enzyme trypsin, pancreatic amylase, and lipase, which digest proteins, carbohydrates, and lipids respectively.
3. Intestinal juice from the intestinal walls contains many enzymes for the complete digestion of all kinds of food.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following is NOT a function of the liver?
skill: Describe the structure and function of the alimentary canal
feedback: The liver is an essential organ that has many functions in the body, including making proteins and blood clotting factors, manufacturing triglycerides and cholesterol, glycogen synthesis, and bile production but it does not produce digestive enzymes. They are mainly produced in the stomach, pancreas, salivary glands, and the small intestine.

subject: Biology
q: The wavelike movement of muscle that pushes food through the digestive system is called _____.
skill: Describe the structure and function of the alimentary canal
feedback: Peristalsis is a series of wave-like muscle contractions that moves food to different processing stations in the digestive tract. The process of peristalsis begins in the oesophagus when a bolus of food is swallowed.

subject: Biology
q: In which of the following organs is urine produced?
skill: State the role of the liver in the metabolism of glucose and amino acids and in the formation of bile juice
feedback: Ammonia is converted into urea in the liver. The urea is transported from the liver to the kidney where it combines with water and other waste substances to form urine. Thus, urine is produced in the kidney which is denoted by the letter C.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following substances produced by the liver would be released into the blood if a person accidentally cuts his finger?
skill: State the role of the liver in the metabolism of glucose and amino acids and in the formation of bile juice
feedback: Whenever a person gets a cut or any other injury that involves the release of blood, the liver releases clotting proteins called fibrinogen that help clot the wound and prevent further bleeding. Therefore, fibrinogen will be released by the liver into the blood if a person cuts his/her finger.

subject: Biology
q: Khalid has had a salad for his lunch which contains tomatoes, lettuce, some beans, and cucumbers. What would be the effect on the digestion of his food if his liver produced insufficient bile?
options: There would be no significant effect.
The rate of digestion would be slowed.
The rate of digestion would be increased.
The provided information is not enough.
skill: State the role of the liver in the metabolism of glucose and amino acids and in the formation of bile juice
feedback: Bile aids in digestion of lipids. A salad will consist of minimal fats and will mostly be carbohydrates and fibre. Therefore, there would be no significant effect on the digestion of his food.

subject: Biology
q: If a patient develops gallstones, the gall bladder may have to be removed. Which of the following processes may be affected by this procedure?
skill: State the role of the liver in the metabolism of glucose and amino acids and in the formation of bile juice
feedback: Bile, secreted by the gallbladder, contains salts that emulsify fats and helps the lipid-digesting enzymes to attack them. Therefore, the digestion of fats will be affected by the removal of the gall bladder.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following organs produces bile?
skill: State the role of the liver in the metabolism of glucose and amino acids and in the formation of bile juice
feedback: Bile is an aqueous secretion produced by the liver which is indicated by the letter A. After its production, bile is stored in the gall bladder.

subject: Biology
q: The conversion of cytosine to uracil (shown below) occurs in the liver. What type of reaction is this?
skill: State the role of the liver in the metabolism of glucose and amino acids and in the formation of bile juice
feedback: The reaction shows that a molecule of water is added to cytosine and a molecule of ammonia is being removed. This reaction occurs in the liver and is called deamination in which an amino group is removed from a molecule.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following organs of our body plays a part in the destruction of old red blood cells (RBCs)?
skill: State the role of the liver in the metabolism of glucose and amino acids and in the formation of bile juice
feedback: Old red blood cells self-destruct in the spleen before passing on to the bone marrow for the production of new cells, or to the liver, where iron is stored in the form of liver. None of the other organs actively destroy or process old red blood cells.

subject: Biology
q: Most digestive processes occur in _____.
skill: Describe the structure and significance of a villus, including the roles of capillaries and lacteals
feedback:
Most digestive processes occur in the duodenum. The small intestine is divided into three parts: duodenum, jejunum, and ileum. The duodenum is the smallest segment of the small intestine; however, most of the digestive processes occur in it. There are three different secretions in the duodenum: bile, pancreatic juice, and intestinal juice.
Bile is secreted by the liver and transported to the duodenum to help in the digestion of lipids. The juice secreted by pancreas contains trypsin, amylase, and lipase. Trypsin breaks down proteins, amylase digests carbohydrates, and lipase digests lipids.
The intestinal juice contains various enzymes that are responsible for digesting all kinds of food.

subject: Biology
q: Villi are present in _____.
skill: Describe the structure and significance of a villus, including the roles of capillaries and lacteals
feedback:
Villi are present in the ileum of the small intestine. The small intestine is divided into three parts: duodenum, jejunum, and ileum. The duodenum is the smallest segment of the small intestine; however, most digestive processes occur in it. The remaining digestive process occurs in jejunum.
Ileum is the last and the largest part of the small intestine. It is responsible for absorbing digested food through villi and microvilli.

subject: Biology
q: The breakdown of large fat globules into tiny fat globules is called _____.
skill: Describe the structure and significance of a villus, including the roles of capillaries and lacteals
feedback:
The breakdown of large fat globules into tiny fat globules is called emulsification. Emulsification results in a higher surface area of fat globules, thereby contributing to a faster rate of digestion. Bile, secreted by the liver and transported to the duodenum, carries out this process.
Lipase is responsible for digesting lipids.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following is responsible for digesting lipids?
skill: Describe the structure and significance of a villus, including the roles of capillaries and lacteals
feedback:
Lipase is responsible for breaking down lipids. The juice secreted by pancreas is transported to the duodenum of the small intestine. It contains trypsin, amylase, and lipase. Trypsin breaks down proteins and amylase digests carbohydrates.

Pepsin is an enzyme responsible for digesting proteins in the stomach. The gastric glands present in the walls of the stomach secrete pepsinogen, an inactive form of pepsin. It is activated due to the action of hydrochloric acid.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following is responsible for digesting carbohydrates?
skill: Describe the structure and significance of a villus, including the roles of capillaries and lacteals
feedback:
Amylase is responsible for breaking down carbohydrates. The juice secreted by pancreas is transported to the duodenum of the small intestine. It contains trypsin, amylase, and lipase.
Trypsin breaks down proteins and lipase digests lipids.
Bile is secreted by the liver and transported to the duodenum to help in the digestion of lipids.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following is responsible for digesting proteins?
skill: Describe the structure and significance of a villus, including the roles of capillaries and lacteals
feedback:
Trypsin is responsible for breaking down proteins. The juice secreted by pancreas is transported to the duodenum of the small intestine. It contains trypsin, amylase, and lipase.
Amylase breaks down carbohydrates and lipase digests lipids.
Bile is secreted by the liver and transported to the duodenum to help in the digestion of lipids.

subject: Biology
q: Where are villi found?
skill: Describe the structure and significance of a villus, including the roles of capillaries and lacteals
feedback: The last 3.5 metres of the small intestine is known as the ileum. It is concerned with the absorption of digested food. There are circular folds in the inner wall of the ileum. These folds have numerous finger-like projections called villi (singular: villus). Villi increase the surface area of the inner walls and help in the absorption of digested food.

subject: Biology
q: According to the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) of the United Nations, more than _____ people die of starvation every day.
skill: Discuss famine with respect to unequal distribution of food, natural disasters, and population growth
feedback: According to the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) of the United Nations, more than 25,000 people die of starvation every day. Each year, roughly 9 million people die of starvation.

subject: Biology
q:
Which of these diseases are common during a famine?
  1. kwashiorkor/li>
  2. marasmus
  3. diabetes
  4. heart diseases
skill: Discuss famine with respect to unequal distribution of food, natural disasters, and population growth
feedback:
Malnutrition is common during famine. Both kwashiorkor and marasmus are deficiency diseases caused by a lack of protein in the diet. Therefore, these diseases are more common or prevalent during famines when food is extremely limited.

On the other hand, diabetes and heart diseases are more likely to occur due to obesity and overconsumption, and therefore would be unlikely to occur during famines. The correct answer is I and II only.

subject: Biology
q:
Which of the following statement(s) is/are true?
  1. Floods increase the fertility of the soil.
  2. Droughts may result in famine.
  3. Global food production decreases every year.
skill: Discuss famine with respect to unequal distribution of food, natural disasters, and population growth
feedback:
The only correct statement is II. This is because floods wash away essential minerals and nutrients from the soil, rendering it infertile and unsuitable for crop production.

Droughts decrease crop yields, thus causing severe food shortages. Persisting low yields of crop end up in causing famine.
Global food production increases every year, however, the disparity in wealth and resources also increases. This results in developed countries having surplus food, while developing countries are afflicted with famine.

subject: Biology
q: WFP stands for _____.
skill: Discuss famine with respect to unequal distribution of food, natural disasters, and population growth
feedback:
WFP stands for World Food Programme.

The World Food Programme is the food-assistance branch of the United Nations. It is the world's largest organisation committed to reducing hunger and increasing food security by providing food to over 80 countries.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following best describes the cause of famine around the world?
skill: Discuss famine with respect to unequal distribution of food, natural disasters, and population growth
feedback: Global food production is increasing every day. However, there is a severe imbalance in the distribution of food, which is the leading cause of famines around the world.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following is the most likely cause of drought?
skill: Discuss famine with respect to unequal distribution of food, natural disasters, and population growth
feedback: When there is low rainfall for a prolonged period, spanning several weeks to years, the flow of water in streams, rivers, and other channels declines. Thus, the water levels in lakes and reservoirs fall. If dry weather persists and water-supply problems develop, the dry spell results in a drought.

subject: Biology
q: Which of these countries was struck by famine between the years 1883-1985?
skill: Discuss famine with respect to unequal distribution of food, natural disasters, and population growth
feedback: Many countries in Africa are stricken by famine or the scarcity of food. Several factors can cause famine, including war, inflation, climate change, crop failure, political and economic irregularities, population imbalance, or government policies.
