These factors can result in the unequal distribution of food and wealth. The correct answer is Ethiopia, which was struck by famine between the years 1883-1985.

subject: Biology
q: Constipation is caused due to_____.
options: excessive absorption of water through duodenum
excessive absorption of water through colon
insufficient absorption of water through colon
insufficient absorption of water through duodenum
skill: State the symptoms, causes, treatment and preventions of the disorders of the gut
feedback: Constipation is caused due to excessive absorption of water through the colon. This hardens the stool, making it difficult to pass. Adding water and fibre to the diet helps prevent constipation.

subject: Biology
q: Ulcers develop when the _____ lining of the stomach gets damaged.
skill: State the symptoms, causes, treatment and preventions of the disorders of the gut
feedback: Ulcers develop when the epithelial lining of the stomach gets damaged. The mucous membrane of the stomach contains simple columnar epithelium tissue which secretes mucous to protect the stomach from acids.

subject: Biology
q: A person who takes aspirin regularly is at risk of developing _____.
skill: State the symptoms, causes, treatment and preventions of the disorders of the gut
feedback: Pain relievers such as aspirin interfere with the stomach's ability to prevent harmful acid attacks. Taking such drugs regularly affects the mucous lining of the stomach, increasing the risk of developing peptic ulcers.

subject: Biology
q: Magnesium hydroxide is a basic compound that neutralizes the effects of acids and is often used as a medicine. Which condition would be treated with this compound?
skill: State the symptoms, causes, treatment and preventions of the disorders of the gut
feedback: Magnesium hydroxide is a basic compound that is a common component of antacids such as milk of magnesia. It neutralizes the stomach acids which can relieve the burning feeling experienced due to ulcers.

subject: Biology
q: Which part of the body can NOT be a site for an ulcer to develop?
options: the inner wall of the small intestine
the inner wall of the large intestine
the inner wall of the kidney
the inner wall of the stomach
skill: State the symptoms, causes, treatment and preventions of the disorders of the gut
feedback: Peptic ulcers occur when acid present in the digestive track starts eating away the inner wall of the gut (oesophagus, small intestine, or stomach). Ulcerative colitis is an inflammatory bowel disease that causes ulcers in the digestive track which also includes negative effects on the inner most lining of the large intestine. Kidney diseases can increase the risk of ulcers but it's not a site for them. So, the correct answer is the inner wall of the kidney.

subject: Biology
q: The ulcers of the stomach are known as a _____.
skill: State the symptoms, causes, treatment and preventions of the disorders of the gut
feedback: Peptic ulcers are open sores that develop on the lining inside the lower oesophagus, stomach, and the duodenum. Therefore, peptic ulcers include gastric, oesophageal, and duodenal ulcers. Ulcers of the stomach, however, are specifically known as gastric ulcers.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following regions of the body can develop peptic ulcers?
skill: State the symptoms, causes, treatment and preventions of the disorders of the gut
feedback: Peptic ulcers are open sores that develop on the lining inside the lower oesophagus, stomach, and the upper portion of the small intestine (also known as the duodenum). In the diagram above, A is the oesophagus, B is the stomach, and D is the duodenum. The correct answer is A, D and B.

subject: Biology
q: Paraffin is an oily substance that is used to relieve _____.
skill: State the symptoms, causes, treatment and preventions of the disorders of the gut
feedback: Paraffin is a transparent, colourless, and oily liquid that is composed of saturated hydrocarbons derived from petroleum. It is used as a laxative for softening the stool and relieving constipation.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following is rarely a cause of constipation?
options: lack of fibre in the diet
skill: State the symptoms, causes, treatment and preventions of the disorders of the gut
feedback: Constipation is rarely caused by viral or bacterial infections and usually occurs as a side effect of medicines or due to diet problems. Having irregularly timed meals also contributes to the hardening of stool, causing constipation.

subject: Biology
q: Diarrhoea can be caused by infection of the _____.
skill: State the symptoms, causes, treatment and preventions of the disorders of the gut
feedback: Diarrhoea can be caused by viral or bacterial infections of the large intestine. It occurs when insufficient water from the stool is absorbed by the large intestine, causing loose and watery bowel movements.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following is the most dangerous symptom of diarrhoea?
skill: State the symptoms, causes, treatment and preventions of the disorders of the gut
feedback: Diarrhoea may not seem that dangerous nowadays, but it can still be fatal if a person becomes too dehydrated. If the amount of water in the body is too low, diarrhoea can become quite dangerous.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following does NOT usually cause diarrhoea?
skill: State the symptoms, causes, treatment and preventions of the disorders of the gut
feedback: Diarrhoea can be caused by viruses and bacteria that are present in contaminated food and water. Diarrhoea can also develop if the hands are not properly washed after contact with contaminated faeces. Contaminated air, however, does not cause diarrhoea.

subject: Biology
q: Diarrhoea is mostly caused by _____ infections.
skill: State the symptoms, causes, treatment and preventions of the disorders of the gut
feedback: Diarrhoea can be caused bybacterial or viral infections. E.coli and salmonella are common bacteria while vibrio cholera and rotavirus are viruses that can cause diarrhoea. Diarrhoea can also be caused by several other factors such as intolerance and allergies to certain foods e.g., lactose intolerance.

subject: Biology
q: Diarrhoea is often accompanied by _____.
skill: State the symptoms, causes, treatment and preventions of the disorders of the gut
feedback: Diarrhoea is often accompanied by nausea and vomiting. The vomiting results in even more water loss in addition to the water bowel movements which can lead to severe dehydration.

subject: Biology
q: Diarrhoea occurs when _____.
options: enough water is not absorbed by the colon
enough water is not absorbed by the duodenum
food is not digested properly by the small intestine
food is not absorbed properly by the small intestine
skill: State the symptoms, causes, treatment and preventions of the disorders of the gut
feedback: Diarrhoea occurs when enough water is not absorbed by the colon. This causes the stool to become watery and loose. Because the body does absorb sufficient water, it becomes dehydrated as a result.

subject: Biology
q: The blood in our arteries is bright red compared to the dull red of the blood in our veins. What is the reason for the change in colour?
options: Blood in veins contains carbon dioxide.
Blood in arteries contains carbon dioxide.
Blood in veins contains a lot of oxygen.
Blood in arteries contains lots of oxygen.
skill: Trace the major pathway of blood through the circulatory system
feedback: The bright red colour of blood in the arteries is due to a high concentration of oxygen. There is a much lower concentration of oxygen in the veins, therefore, this blood has a dull red colour.

subject: Biology
q: Veins have low blood pressure, so they need _____ to make sure the blood flows in only one direction. Arteries only need them at the point of exit from the heart.
skill: Trace the major pathway of blood through the circulatory system
feedback: There is much lower blood pressure in veins than in arteries, therefore veins require valves to keep the blood flowing in only one direction. Arteries, however, carry blood with high pressure and only require valves when leaving the heart to maintain one directional flow.

subject: Biology
q: Haemoglobin is a protein that allows _____ to carry oxygen.
skill: Trace the major pathway of blood through the circulatory system
feedback: Red blood cells are shaped like a biconcave disc. Haemoglobin is a protein that combines with oxygen and transport oxygen all over the body.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following veins carry blood from the kidneys?
skill: Trace the major pathway of blood through the circulatory system
feedback: The renal arteries branch off of the abdominal aorta and supply the kidneys with blood. The arterial supply of the kidneys varies from person to person, and there may be one or more renal arteries to supply each kidney. The renal veins are the veins that drain blood from venules that arise from the interlobular capillaries inside the parenchyma of the kidney and connect them to the inferior vena cava.

subject: Biology
q: The atria are separated from each other by a muscular wall. The atria are connected to ventricles by _____.
skill: Describe the external and internal structure of the human heart with reference to its function
feedback: The atria are two upper chambers of the heart. The atria contracts to force blood into the ventricles through a valve. The valves ensure one-directional flow when the ventricles contract to send blood to the lungs and the rest of the body.

subject: Biology
q: Blood vessels called _____ carry blood to the two atria.
skill: Describe the external and internal structure of the human heart with reference to its function
feedback:
The heart has two types of blood vessels. Those that carry blood from the heart and those carry blood to the heart. The vessels that carry blood back to the heart are called veins.
Pulmonary vein brings the blood back from the lungs and the venae cavae bring the blood back from the rest of the body.

subject: Biology
q: Which chamber of the heart delivers blood to the rest of the body?
skill: Describe the external and internal structure of the human heart with reference to its function
feedback: The heart receives oxygenated blood from the left atrium. Blood then goes into the left ventricle to be further transported to the rest of the body.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following chambers has the thickest walls in the human heart?
skill: Describe the external and internal structure of the human heart with reference to its function
feedback: The left ventricle has a thicker muscle wall than the right ventricle. This is because the left ventricle has to pump blood all the way around the body, but the right ventricle only has to pump it to the lungs. The blood in the arteries is under higher pressure compared to the blood in the veins.

subject: Biology
q: Atria and ventricles relax and blood fills the atria during _____.
skill: Define the terms heartbeat, heart rate and pulse rate
feedback: During cardiac diastole, the atria and ventricles relax and the blood coming from the lungs fills the atria.

subject: Biology
q: One heartbeat equals ____ complete cardiac cycle.
skill: Define the terms heartbeat, heart rate and pulse rate
feedback: One complete cardiac cycle is equal to one heartbeat. It involves the alternating contraction and relaxation of the atria and ventricles.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following contains deoxygenated blood in an adult human?
skill: Define the terms heartbeat, heart rate and pulse rate
feedback:
Arteries carry oxygenated blood away from the heart to the tissues, except for pulmonary arteries, which carry blood to the lungs for oxygenation.

Veins usually carry deoxygenated blood to the heart, but the pulmonary veins carry oxygenated blood as well.

subject: Biology
q: When do the atria contract?
skill: Define the terms heartbeat, heart rate and pulse rate
feedback: In one heartbeat of a continuously repeating cardia-cycle, the atria and ventricles relax, and blood is filled in the atria. This period is called cardiac diastole. Immediately after their filling, both atria contract and pump blood towards the ventricle. This period in the cardiac cycle is called atrial systole.

subject: Biology
q: In heart transplants, the donor is someone who has been declared _____ but remains on life support.
skill: State the causes, symptoms, treatment and preventions of myocardial infarction
feedback: Heart transplant are complicated surgeries. To find a match, the donor must be on life support but is considered brain dead after attempts to treat the patient have failed. Thus, the possibility of finding suitable donors for heart transplant surgeries is very rare.

subject: Biology
q: A good diet is important for reducing the risk of atherosclerosis because _____ tends to collect on the walls of the coronary arteries.
skill: State the causes, symptoms, treatment and preventions of myocardial infarction
feedback: People with bad eating habits have a higher chance of heart disease because excess fat tends to collect in the arteries. This disturbs the blood flow to the heart, which in turn can lead to heart attacks.

subject: Biology
q: The coronary arteries supply the heart muscle with food and oxygen. If _____ clogs the arteries because of a bad diet, the heart becomes weaker and may stop beating.
skill: State the causes, symptoms, treatment and preventions of myocardial infarction
feedback: Cholesterol causes the narrowing of the coronary arteries. This narrowing of the coronary arteries is called coronary artery disease or atherosclerosis.

subject: Biology
q: A patient suffering from a blood disorder visits a doctor. To determine the blood disorder, the doctor orders various blood tests. The results suggest that the patient's red blood cells are abnormal but the white blood cells have normal shape and functioning. Identify the type of blood disorder that the patient is suffering from.
skill: State symptoms causes and treatment of the diseases of blood (leukaemia and thalassemia)
feedback:
A patient who has abnormal red blood cells is either suffering from thalassaemia or sickle cell anaemia. However, not enough information is given to distinguish between these two disorders.

The functioning of the red blood cells is affected in both thalassaemia and sickle cell anaemia.
Leukaemia results ONLY in the production of abnormal white blood cells. The red blood cells function normally in individuals suffering from leukaemia.

subject: Biology
q: Identify the cells that are affected in thalassaemia.
skill: State symptoms causes and treatment of the diseases of blood (leukaemia and thalassemia)
feedback:
Only red blood cells are affected in thalassaemia. Image A is that of red blood cells.
Image B shows platelets that play a role in the process of blood clotting.
Image C is that of lymphocytes while image D displays monocytes. Both are types of white blood cells that become abnormal in leukaemia.

subject: Biology
q: Identify the cells that are affected in leukaemia.
skill: State symptoms causes and treatment of the diseases of blood (leukaemia and thalassemia)
feedback:
Only white blood cells are affected in leukaemia. Image C is that of lymphocytes while image D displays monocytes. Both are types of white blood cells that become abnormal in leukaemia.

Image A is that of red blood cells while image B shows platelets. Neither of these is affected in leukaemia.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following cells are affected in thalassaemia?
skill: State symptoms causes and treatment of the diseases of blood (leukaemia and thalassemia)
feedback: The function of erythrocytes is affected in thalassaemia. It is a hereditary disease in which the gene for haemoglobin is mutated. Haemoglobin is a protein found in red blood cells that is involved in the distribution of oxygen. Therefore, erythrocytes or red blood cells cannot properly transport oxygen throughout the body.

subject: Biology
q: Thalassaemia and leukaemia be treated through _____.
skill: State symptoms causes and treatment of the diseases of blood (leukaemia and thalassemia)
feedback:
The treatment of thalassaemia and leukaemia involves chemotherapy.
Blood transfusions alone are not enough to treat these diseases.
Although genetic engineering may someday be used as a treatment or a cure for thalassaemia and leukaemia, no such treatments exist currently.
Antibiotics are used to treat bacterial infections only.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following diseases is hereditary?
skill: State symptoms causes and treatment of the diseases of blood (leukaemia and thalassemia)
feedback: Thalassaemia is a disease in which the gene for haemoglobin is mutated. Thalassaemia is considered a hereditary disease because it can be passed on by family members.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following diseases is NOT hereditary?
skill: State symptoms causes and treatment of the diseases of blood (leukaemia and thalassemia)
feedback: Leukaemia is NOT a hereditary disease; it occurs due to genetic mutations. Most Leukaemia cases are not considered hereditary because they cannot be passed on by family members.

subject: Biology
q: Thalassaemia can be cured through _____.
skill: State symptoms causes and treatment of the diseases of blood (leukaemia and thalassemia)
feedback:
Thalassaemia is a hereditary disease that can ONLY be cured through bone marrow transplants.

Mutations in the gene for haemoglobin alters the structure and function of haemoglobin (transporting oxygen throughout the body), thus causing thalassaemia.

subject: Biology
q: Haemoglobin is defective in individuals suffering from thalassaemia due to _____
options: a mutation in the gene of haemoglobin.
to environmental hazards, such as toxins.
the absence of nucleus in red blood cells.
skill: State symptoms causes and treatment of the diseases of blood (leukaemia and thalassemia)
feedback: Thalassaemia is a hereditary disease in which the gene for haemoglobin is mutated. Therefore, the structure and function of haemoglobin (transporting oxygen throughout the body) is compromised.

subject: Biology
q: Which molecule is affected in thalassaemia?
skill: State symptoms causes and treatment of the diseases of blood (leukaemia and thalassemia)
feedback: Thalassaemia is a hereditary disease in which the gene for haemoglobin is mutated. Therefore, the structure and function of haemoglobin (transporting oxygen throughout the body) is compromised.

subject: Biology
q: How can leukaemia be cured?
skill: State symptoms causes and treatment of the diseases of blood (leukaemia and thalassemia)
feedback: Leukaemia can be cured through bone marrow transplants. A cancerous mutation in the bone marrow or lymph tissues results in the production of numerous immature and abnormal white blood cells. The treatment of leukaemia involves chemotherapy and blood transfusions. However, bone marrow transplants are the only known cure.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following is TRUE?
options: Leukaemia patients are more prone to infections.
One of the symptoms of leukaemia is hair fall.
Leukaemia affects red blood cells in humans.
Leukaemia can be cured through antibiotics.
skill: State symptoms causes and treatment of the diseases of blood (leukaemia and thalassemia)
feedback:
The correct response is: Leukaemia patients are more prone to infections.

A cancerous mutation in the bone marrow or lymph tissues results in the production of numerous immature and abnormal white blood cells. Since white blood cells are involved in protecting the body against germs, abnormal white blood cells would make an individual more prone to infections.
